infobox.value: ([/ˌɪlɪˈnɔɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Illinois.ogg "File:En-us-Illinois.ogg") [*IL-ih-NOY*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the [Midwestern](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") region of the United States. It borders on [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") to its northeast, the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") to its west, and the [Wabash](/wiki/Wabash_River "Wabash River") and [Ohio rivers](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") to its south.[[b]](#cite_note-7) Of the fifty U.S. states, Illinois has the [fifth-largest gross domestic product](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_GDP "List of U.S. states and territories by GDP") (GDP), the [sixth-largest population](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population"), and the [25th-most land area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area"). Its capital city is [Springfield](/wiki/Springfield,_Illinois "Springfield, Illinois") in the center of the state, and the state's largest city is [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") in the northeast.

**Illinois** ([/ˌɪlɪˈnɔɪ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Illinois.ogg "File:En-us-Illinois.ogg") [*IL-ih-NOY*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the [Midwestern](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") region of the United States. It borders on [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") to its northeast, the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") to its west, and the [Wabash](/wiki/Wabash_River "Wabash River") and [Ohio rivers](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") to its south.[[b]](#cite_note-7) Of the fifty U.S. states, Illinois has the [fifth-largest gross domestic product](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_GDP "List of U.S. states and territories by GDP") (GDP), the [sixth-largest population](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population"), and the [25th-most land area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area"). Its capital city is [Springfield](/wiki/Springfield,_Illinois "Springfield, Illinois") in the center of the state, and the state's largest city is [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") in the northeast.

Present-day Illinois was inhabited by [Indigenous cultures](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas#History "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") for thousands of years. The French were the first Europeans to arrive, settling near the Mississippi and [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois_River "Illinois River") rivers in the 17th century [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country"), as part of their sprawling colony of [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"). A century later, the revolutionary war [Illinois campaign](/wiki/Illinois_campaign "Illinois campaign") prefigured American involvement in the region. Following [U.S. independence in 1783](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"), which made the Mississippi River the national boundary, American settlers began arriving from [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky") via the Ohio River. Illinois was soon part of the United States' oldest territory, the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory"), and in 1818 it achieved [statehood](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_date_of_admission_to_the_Union "List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union"). The [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") brought increased commercial activity in the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes"), and the invention of the self-scouring [steel plow](/wiki/Plough "Plough") by Illinoisan [John Deere](/wiki/John_Deere_(inventor) "John Deere (inventor)") turned the state's rich [prairie](/wiki/Prairie "Prairie") into some of the world's most productive and valuable farmland, attracting [immigrant](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") farmers from [Germany](/wiki/German_American "German American"), [Sweden](/wiki/Swedish_American "Swedish American") and elsewhere.

In the mid-19th century, the [Illinois and Michigan Canal](/wiki/Illinois_and_Michigan_Canal "Illinois and Michigan Canal") and a sprawling railroad network facilitated trade, commerce, and settlement, making the state a transportation hub for the nation, especially in Chicago, which became the world's fastest growing city by the late 19th century.[[6]](#cite_note-8) By 1900, the growth of industrial jobs in the northern cities and [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") mining in the central and southern areas attracted immigrants from [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and [Southern Europe](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe"). Illinois became one of America's most industrialized states and remains a major manufacturing center.[[7]](#cite_note-9) The [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") from the South established a large Black community, particularly in Chicago, which became a leading cultural, economic, and population center; the [Chicago metropolitan area](/wiki/Chicago_metropolitan_area "Chicago metropolitan area") holds about 65% of the state's 12.8 million residents.

Two [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") are in Illinois: the ancient [Cahokia Mounds](/wiki/Cahokia_Mounds "Cahokia Mounds"), and part of the [Wright architecture](/wiki/The_20th-Century_Architecture_of_Frank_Lloyd_Wright "The 20th-Century Architecture of Frank Lloyd Wright") site. A wide variety of [protected areas](/wiki/List_of_protected_areas_of_Illinois "List of protected areas of Illinois") seek to conserve Illinois' natural and cultural resources. Major centers of learning include the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"), [University of Illinois](/wiki/University_of_Illinois "University of Illinois"), and [Northwestern University](/wiki/Northwestern_University "Northwestern University"). Three [U.S. presidents](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") have been elected while residents of Illinois: [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"), [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant"), and [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"); additionally, [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") was born and raised in the state, though he served for [California](/wiki/California "California"). Illinois honors Lincoln with its official state slogan *Land of Lincoln*.[[8]](#cite_note-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11) The state is the site of the [Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library and Museum](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln_Presidential_Library_and_Museum "Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library and Museum") in Springfield and the future home of the [Barack Obama Presidential Center](/wiki/Barack_Obama_Presidential_Center "Barack Obama Presidential Center") in Chicago.

The [economy of Illinois](/wiki/Economy_of_Illinois "Economy of Illinois") is highly diverse, with the [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city") of Chicago in the northeast, major industrial and [agricultural](/wiki/Agricultural_productivity "Agricultural productivity") hubs in the north and center, and [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resource "Natural resource") such as coal, timber, and petroleum in the south. Owing to its central location and favorable geography, the state is a major [transportation hub](/wiki/Transportation_hub "Transportation hub"): the [Port of Chicago](/wiki/Port_of_Chicago "Port of Chicago") has access to the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") through the Great Lakes and [Saint Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_Seaway "Saint Lawrence Seaway") and to the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") from the Mississippi River via the [Illinois Waterway](/wiki/Illinois_Waterway "Illinois Waterway"). Chicago has been the nation's railroad hub since the 1860s,[[10]](#cite_note-12) and its [O'Hare International Airport](/wiki/O%27Hare_International_Airport "O'Hare International Airport") has been among the world's busiest airports for decades. Illinois has long been considered a [microcosm](/wiki/Macrocosm_and_microcosm "Macrocosm and microcosm") of the United States and a [bellwether](/wiki/Bellwether_(politics) "Bellwether (politics)") in American culture, exemplified by the phrase *[Will it play in Peoria?](/wiki/Will_it_play_in_Peoria%3F "Will it play in Peoria?")*.[[11]](#cite_note-so-13)

## Etymology

See also: [Illinois Confederation](/wiki/Illinois_Confederation "Illinois Confederation") and [List of counties in Illinois](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_Illinois "List of counties in Illinois")

"Illinois" is the modern spelling for the early [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") [Catholic](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") [missionaries](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") and explorers' name for the [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois_Confederation "Illinois Confederation") [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), a name that was spelled in many different ways in the early records.[[12]](#cite_note-14)

American scholars previously thought the name *Illinois* meant 'man' or 'men' in the [Miami-Illinois language](/wiki/Miami-Illinois_language "Miami-Illinois language"), with the original *iliniwek* transformed via French into Illinois.[[13]](#cite_note-15)[[14]](#cite_note-16) This etymology is not supported by the Illinois language,[[15]](#cite_note-17) as the word for "man" is *ireniwa*, and plural of "man" is *ireniwaki*. The name *[Illiniwek](/wiki/Illiniwek "Illiniwek")* has also been said to mean 'tribe of superior men',[[16]](#cite_note-18) which is a [false etymology](/wiki/False_etymology "False etymology"). The name *Illinois* derives from the Miami-Illinois verb *irenwe·wa* 'he speaks the regular way'. This was taken into the [Ojibwe language](/wiki/Ojibwe_language "Ojibwe language"), perhaps in the [Ottawa dialect](/wiki/Ottawa_dialect "Ottawa dialect"), and modified into *ilinwe·* (pluralized as *ilinwe·k*). The French borrowed these forms, spelling the /we/ ending as *-ois*, a [transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") of that sound in the French of that time. The current spelling form, *Illinois*, began to appear in the early 1670s, when French colonists had settled in the western area. The Illinois's name for themselves, as attested in all three of the French missionary-period dictionaries of Illinois, was *[Inoka](/wiki/Inoka "Inoka")*, of unknown meaning and unrelated to the other terms.[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20)

## History

Main article: [History of Illinois](/wiki/History_of_Illinois "History of Illinois")

### Pre-European

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Upper_Bluff_Lake_Dancing_Figures_plate_HRoe_2012.jpg/250px-Upper_Bluff_Lake_Dancing_Figures_plate_HRoe_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Upper_Bluff_Lake_Dancing_Figures_plate_HRoe_2012.jpg)

[Mississippian copper plate](/wiki/Mississippian_copper_plate "Mississippian copper plate") found at the Saddle Site in [Union County, Illinois](/wiki/Union_County,_Illinois "Union County, Illinois")

[American Indians](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") of successive cultures lived along the waterways of the Illinois area for thousands of years before the arrival of Europeans. The [Koster Site](/wiki/Koster_Site "Koster Site") has been excavated and demonstrates 7,000 years of continuous habitation. [Cahokia](/wiki/Cahokia "Cahokia"), the largest regional [chiefdom](/wiki/Chiefdom "Chiefdom") and [urban center](/wiki/Urban_center "Urban center") of the [pre-Columbian](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_era "Pre-Columbian era") [Mississippian culture](/wiki/Mississippian_culture "Mississippian culture"), was located near present-day [Collinsville, Illinois](/wiki/Collinsville,_Illinois "Collinsville, Illinois"). They built an urban complex of more than 100 [platform](/wiki/Platform_mound "Platform mound") and [burial mounds](/wiki/Burial_mound "Burial mound"), a 50-acre (20 ha) [plaza](/wiki/Plaza "Plaza") larger than 35 football fields,[[19]](#cite_note-PAUKETATCAHOKIA-21) and a woodhenge of sacred cedar, all in a planned design expressing the culture's cosmology. [Monks Mound](/wiki/Monks_Mound "Monks Mound"), the center of the site, is the largest Pre-Columbian structure north of the [Valley of Mexico](/wiki/Valley_of_Mexico "Valley of Mexico"). It is 100 ft (30 m) high, 951 ft (290 m) long, 836 ft (255 m) wide, and covers 13.8 acres (5.6 ha).[[20]](#cite_note-SKELE-22) It contains about 814,000 cu yd (622,000 m3) of earth.[[21]](#cite_note-SNOW2010-23) It was topped by a structure thought to have measured about 105 ft (32 m) in length and 48 ft (15 m) in width, covered an area 5,000 sq ft (460 m2), and been as much as 50 ft (15 m) high, making its peak 150 ft (46 m) above the level of the plaza. The finely crafted ornaments and tools recovered by archaeologists at [Cahokia](/wiki/Cahokia "Cahokia") include elaborate ceramics, finely sculptured stonework, carefully embossed and engraved copper and [mica](/wiki/Mica "Mica") sheets, and one funeral blanket for an important chief fashioned from 20,000 shell beads. These artifacts indicate that Cahokia was truly an urban center, with clustered housing, markets, and specialists in toolmaking, hide dressing, potting, jewelry making, shell engraving, weaving and salt making.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

The civilization vanished in the 15th century for unknown reasons, but historians and archeologists have speculated that the people depleted the area of resources. Many indigenous tribes engaged in constant warfare. According to Suzanne Austin Alchon, "At one site in the central [Illinois River](/wiki/Illinois_River "Illinois River") valley, one third of all adults died as a result of violent injuries."[[23]](#cite_note-25) The next major power in the region was the [Illinois Confederation](/wiki/Illinois_Confederation "Illinois Confederation") or Illini, a political alliance.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Around the time of European contact in 1673, the Illinois confederation had an estimated population of over 10,000 people.[[25]](#cite_note-museum.state.il.us-27) As the Illini declined during the [Beaver Wars](/wiki/Beaver_Wars "Beaver Wars") era, members of the [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages")-speaking [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi"), [Miami](/wiki/Miami_people "Miami people"), [Sauk](/wiki/Sauk_people "Sauk people"), and other tribes including the [Meskwaki](/wiki/Meskwaki "Meskwaki"), [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa_people "Iowa people"), [Kickapoo](/wiki/Kickapoo "Kickapoo"), [Mascouten](/wiki/Mascouten "Mascouten"), [Piankeshaw](/wiki/Piankeshaw "Piankeshaw"), [Shawnee](/wiki/Shawnee "Shawnee"), [Wea](/wiki/Wea "Wea"), and [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk") came into the area from the east and north around the Great Lakes.[[26]](#cite_note-Nelson-28)[[27]](#cite_note-29)

### European exploration and settlement prior to 1800

Main articles: [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"); [Louisiana (New France)](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)"); [Canada (New France)](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)"); [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country"); [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"); [Treaty of Paris (1763)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"); [Province of Quebec (1763–1791)](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)"); [Indian Reserve (1763)](/wiki/Indian_Reserve_(1763) "Indian Reserve (1763)"); [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"); [Western theater of the American Revolutionary War](/wiki/Western_theater_of_the_American_Revolutionary_War "Western theater of the American Revolutionary War"); [Illinois County, Virginia](/wiki/Illinois_County,_Virginia "Illinois County, Virginia"); [Treaty of Paris (1783)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"); [Northwest Ordinance](/wiki/Northwest_Ordinance "Northwest Ordinance"); and [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Illinois_1718.jpg/250px-Illinois_1718.jpg)](/wiki/File:Illinois_1718.jpg)

Illinois in 1718, approximate modern state area highlighted, from *Carte de la Louisiane et du cours du Mississipi* by [Guillaume de L'Isle](/wiki/Guillaume_de_L%27Isle "Guillaume de L'Isle")[[28]](#cite_note-30)

French explorers [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette") and [Louis Jolliet](/wiki/Louis_Jolliet "Louis Jolliet") explored the [Illinois River](/wiki/Illinois_River "Illinois River") in 1673. Marquette soon after founded a mission at the [Grand Village of the Illinois](/wiki/Grand_Village_of_the_Illinois "Grand Village of the Illinois") in [Illinois Country](/wiki/Illinois_Country "Illinois Country"). In 1680, French explorers under [René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9-Robert_Cavelier,_Sieur_de_La_Salle "René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle") and [Henri de Tonti](/wiki/Henri_de_Tonti "Henri de Tonti") constructed a fort at the site of present-day [Peoria](/wiki/Peoria,_Illinois "Peoria, Illinois"), and in 1682, a fort atop [Starved Rock](/wiki/Starved_Rock "Starved Rock") in today's Starved Rock State Park. French Empire [Canadiens](/wiki/Canadiens "Canadiens") came south to settle particularly along the Mississippi River, and Illinois was part of first [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"), and then of [La Louisiane](/wiki/La_Louisiane "La Louisiane") until 1763, when it passed to the British with their defeat of France in the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"). The small French settlements continued, although many French migrated west to [Ste. Genevieve](/wiki/Ste._Genevieve,_Missouri "Ste. Genevieve, Missouri") and [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri"), to evade British rule.[[29]](#cite_note-Biles-31)

A few British soldiers were posted in Illinois, but few British or American settlers moved there, as the Crown made it part of the territory reserved for Indians west of the Appalachians, and then part of the [British Province of Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)"). In 1778, [George Rogers Clark](/wiki/George_Rogers_Clark "George Rogers Clark") claimed [Illinois County](/wiki/Illinois_County "Illinois County") for [Virginia](/wiki/Colony_of_Virginia "Colony of Virginia"). In a compromise, Virginia (and other states that made various claims) ceded the area to the new United States in the 1780s and it became part of the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory"), administered by the federal government and later organized as states.[[29]](#cite_note-Biles-31)

### 19th century

Main articles: [Indiana Territory](/wiki/Indiana_Territory "Indiana Territory"), [Organic act § List of organic acts](/wiki/Organic_act#List_of_organic_acts "Organic act"), [Illinois Territory](/wiki/Illinois_Territory "Illinois Territory"), [Admission to the Union](/wiki/Admission_to_the_Union "Admission to the Union"), and [List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_date_of_admission_to_the_Union "List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union")

See also: [History of Chicago](/wiki/History_of_Chicago "History of Chicago") and [History of Nauvoo, Illinois](/wiki/History_of_Nauvoo,_Illinois "History of Nauvoo, Illinois")

#### Prior to statehood

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Kaskaskia_Bell_3321.jpg/250px-Kaskaskia_Bell_3321.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kaskaskia_Bell_3321.jpg)

The bell donated by King Louis XV in 1741 to the French mission at Kaskaskia. It was later called the "Liberty Bell of the West", after it was rung to celebrate U.S. victory in the Revolution

The [Illinois-Wabash Company](/wiki/Illinois-Wabash_Company "Illinois-Wabash Company") was an early claimant to much of Illinois. The [Illinois Territory](/wiki/Illinois_Territory "Illinois Territory") was created on February 3, 1809, with its capital at [Kaskaskia](/wiki/Kaskaskia,_Illinois "Kaskaskia, Illinois"), an early French settlement.

During the discussions leading up to Illinois's [admission to the Union](/wiki/Admission_to_the_Union "Admission to the Union"), the proposed northern boundary of the state was moved twice.[[30]](#cite_note-32) The original provisions of the [Northwest Ordinance](/wiki/Northwest_Ordinance "Northwest Ordinance") had specified a boundary that would have been tangent to the southern tip of Lake Michigan. Such a boundary would have left Illinois with no shoreline on Lake Michigan at all. However, as Indiana had successfully been granted a 10 mi (16 km) northern extension of its boundary to provide it with a usable lakefront, the original bill for Illinois statehood, submitted to Congress on January 23, 1818, stipulated a northern border at the same latitude as Indiana's, which is defined as 10 miles north of the southernmost extremity of Lake Michigan. However, the Illinois delegate, [Nathaniel Pope](/wiki/Nathaniel_Pope "Nathaniel Pope"), wanted more, and lobbied to have the boundary moved further north. The final bill passed by Congress included an amendment to shift the border to 42° 30' north, which is approximately 51 mi (82 km) north of the Indiana northern border. This shift added 8,500 sq mi (22,000 km2) to the state, including the [lead mining](/wiki/Lead_mining "Lead mining") region near [Galena](/wiki/Galena,_Illinois "Galena, Illinois"). More importantly, it added nearly 50 miles of Lake Michigan shoreline and the Chicago River. Pope and others envisioned a canal that would connect the Chicago and Illinois rivers and thus connect the Great Lakes to the Mississippi.

#### State of Illinois prior to the Civil War

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/United_States_1818-12-1819-03.png/250px-United_States_1818-12-1819-03.png)](/wiki/File:United_States_1818-12-1819-03.png)

In 1818, Illinois became the 21st U.S. state. The southern portion of [Illinois Territory](/wiki/Illinois_Territory "Illinois Territory") was admitted as the state of Illinois, and the rest was joined to [Michigan Territory](/wiki/Michigan_Territory "Michigan Territory").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Edward_Coles.png/250px-Edward_Coles.png)](/wiki/File:Edward_Coles.png)

The second [Governor of Illinois](/wiki/Governor_of_Illinois "Governor of Illinois"), [Edward Coles](/wiki/Edward_Coles "Edward Coles") brought his slaves from his home state of [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") to give them their [freedom](/wiki/Manumission "Manumission") when they arrived in Illinois.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Springfield%2CIllinois-Old_State_Capitol.jpg/250px-Springfield%2CIllinois-Old_State_Capitol.jpg)](/wiki/File:Springfield,Illinois-Old_State_Capitol.jpg)

[Old State Capitol](/wiki/Old_State_Capitol_State_Historic_Site "Old State Capitol State Historic Site"): Abraham Lincoln and other area legislators were instrumental in moving the state capitol to centrally located Springfield in 1839.

In 1818, Illinois became the 21st U.S. state. The capital remained at Kaskaskia, headquartered in a small building rented by the state. In 1819, [Vandalia](/wiki/Vandalia,_Illinois "Vandalia, Illinois") became the capital, and over the next 18 years, three separate buildings were built to serve successively as the capitol building. In 1837, the state legislators representing [Sangamon County](/wiki/Sangamon_County "Sangamon County"), under the leadership of state representative [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"), succeeded in having the capital moved to [Springfield](/wiki/Springfield,_Illinois "Springfield, Illinois"),[[31]](#cite_note-33) where a [fifth capitol](/wiki/Old_State_Capitol_State_Historic_Site_(Illinois) "Old State Capitol State Historic Site (Illinois)") building was constructed. A [sixth capitol](/wiki/Illinois_State_Capitol "Illinois State Capitol") building was erected in 1867, which continues to serve as the Illinois capitol today.

Though it was ostensibly a "[free state](/wiki/Slave_and_free_states "Slave and free states")", there was nonetheless [slavery in Illinois](/wiki/History_of_slavery_in_Illinois "History of slavery in Illinois"). The ethnic French had owned black slaves since the 1720s, and American settlers had already brought slaves into the area from [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"). Slavery was nominally banned by the Northwest Ordinance, but that was not enforced for those already holding slaves. When Illinois became a state in 1818, the Ordinance no longer applied, and about 900 slaves were held in the state. As the southern part of the state, later known as "Egypt" or "Little Egypt",[[32]](#cite_note-34)[[33]](#cite_note-35) was largely settled by migrants from the South, the section was hostile to free blacks. [Edward Coles](/wiki/Edward_Coles "Edward Coles"), the second Governor of Illinois who was born in Virginia, participated in a campaign to block extending existing slavery in Illinois after winning the [1822 Illinois gubernatorial election](/wiki/1822_Illinois_gubernatorial_election "1822 Illinois gubernatorial election"). In 1824, state residents voted against making slavery legal by a vote of 6640 against to 4972 for.[[34]](#cite_note-36)

Still, most residents opposed allowing free blacks as permanent residents. Some settlers brought in slaves seasonally or as house servants.[[35]](#cite_note-37) The [Illinois Constitution of 1848](/wiki/Constitution_of_Illinois "Constitution of Illinois") was written with a provision for exclusionary laws to be passed. In 1853, [John A. Logan](/wiki/John_A._Logan "John A. Logan") helped pass a law to prohibit all African Americans, including [freedmen](/wiki/Freedman "Freedman"), from settling in the state.[[36]](#cite_note-38)

The [winter of 1830–1831](/w/index.php?title=Hard_Winter_of_1880%E2%80%931881&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hard Winter of 1880–1881 (page does not exist)") is called the "Winter of the Deep Snow";[[37]](#cite_note-39) a sudden, deep snowfall blanketed the state, making travel impossible for the rest of the winter, and many travelers perished. Several severe winters followed, including the "Winter of the Sudden Freeze". On December 20, 1836, a fast-moving cold front passed through, freezing puddles in minutes and killing many travelers who could not reach shelter. The adverse weather resulted in crop failures in the northern part of the state. The southern part of the state shipped food north, and this may have contributed to its name, "[Little Egypt](/wiki/Southern_Illinois "Southern Illinois")", after the Biblical story of [Joseph in Egypt](/wiki/Joseph_(Genesis) "Joseph (Genesis)") supplying grain to his brothers.[[38]](#cite_note-40)

In 1832, the [Black Hawk War](/wiki/Black_Hawk_War "Black Hawk War") was fought in Illinois and present-day [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") between the United States and a coalition of the [Sauk](/wiki/Sauk_people "Sauk people"), [Meskwaki](/wiki/Meskwaki "Meskwaki"), and [Kickapoo](/wiki/Kickapoo "Kickapoo") peoples, who had been forced to leave their homes and relocate to Iowa in 1831. The tribes had lost their territory east of the Mississippi river in Illinois under a [disputed treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_St._Louis_(1804) "Treaty of St. Louis (1804)") in 1804. The natives, under Sauk chief [Black Hawk](/wiki/Black_Hawk_(Sauk_leader) "Black Hawk (Sauk leader)"), attempted to return to Illinois in April 1832 to reclaim this land. They were attacked and defeated by the [U.S. Militia](/wiki/Militia_(United_States) "Militia (United States)") and rival tribes allied with the US forces, including the [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi"), [Dakota](/wiki/Dakota_people "Dakota people"), [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee"), and [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk"). The survivors of Black Hawk's band were forced back to Iowa.[[39]](#cite_note-41) This represented the end of native resistance to white settlement in the Chicago and Northern Illinois regions.[[40]](#cite_note-42) By 1832, when the last native lands in Illinois were ceded to the United States, the indigenous population of the state had been reduced by infectious diseases, warfare, and [forced westward removal](/wiki/Indian_removal "Indian removal") to only one village with fewer than 300 inhabitants.[[25]](#cite_note-museum.state.il.us-27)

By 1839, the [Latter Day Saints](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saints "Latter Day Saints") had founded a [utopian](/wiki/Utopian "Utopian") city called [Nauvoo](/wiki/Nauvoo,_Illinois "Nauvoo, Illinois"), formerly called Commerce. Located in [Hancock County](/wiki/Hancock_County,_Illinois "Hancock County, Illinois") along the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), Nauvoo flourished and, by 1844, briefly surpassed Chicago for the position of the state's largest city.[[41]](#cite_note-43)[[42]](#cite_note-44) But in that same year, the [Latter Day Saint movement](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Latter Day Saint movement") founder, [Joseph Smith](/wiki/Joseph_Smith "Joseph Smith"), [was killed](/wiki/Death_of_Joseph_Smith "Death of Joseph Smith") in the [Carthage Jail](/wiki/Carthage_Jail "Carthage Jail"), about 30 miles away from Nauvoo. Following a [succession crisis](/wiki/Succession_crisis_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Succession crisis (Latter Day Saints)"), [Brigham Young](/wiki/Brigham_Young "Brigham Young") led most Latter Day Saints out of Illinois in a [mass exodus](/wiki/Mormon_pioneers "Mormon pioneers") to present-day [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"); after close to six years of rapid development, Nauvoo quickly declined afterward.

After it was established in 1833, [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") gained prominence as a [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") port, and then as an [Illinois and Michigan Canal](/wiki/Illinois_and_Michigan_Canal "Illinois and Michigan Canal") port after 1848, and as a rail hub soon afterward. By 1857, Chicago was Illinois's largest city.[[29]](#cite_note-Biles-31) With the tremendous growth of mines and factories in the state in the 19th century, Illinois was the ground for the formation of [labor unions in the United States](/wiki/Labor_unions_in_the_United_States "Labor unions in the United States").

In 1847, after lobbying by [Dorothea L. Dix](/wiki/Dorothea_Dix "Dorothea Dix"), Illinois became one of the first states to establish a system of state-supported treatment of mental illness and disabilities, replacing local [almshouses](/wiki/Almshouse "Almshouse"). Dix came into this effort after having met J. O. King, a [Jacksonville, Illinois](/wiki/Jacksonville,_Illinois "Jacksonville, Illinois") businessman, who invited her to Illinois, where he had been working to build an asylum for the insane. With the lobbying expertise of Dix, plans for the Jacksonville State Hospital (now known as the [Jacksonville Developmental Center](/wiki/Jacksonville_Developmental_Center "Jacksonville Developmental Center")) were signed into law on March 1, 1847.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

#### Civil War and after

Main article: [Illinois in the American Civil War](/wiki/Illinois_in_the_American_Civil_War "Illinois in the American Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Embarkation_of_General_McClernand%27s_Brigade_at_Cairo.jpg/250px-Embarkation_of_General_McClernand%27s_Brigade_at_Cairo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Embarkation_of_General_McClernand%27s_Brigade_at_Cairo.jpg)

[Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") troops embarking at [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo,_Illinois "Cairo, Illinois") on January 10, 1862

During the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), Illinois ranked fourth in soldiers who served (more than 250,000) in the [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army"), a figure surpassed by only New York, [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), and [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"). Beginning with President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln")'s first call for troops and continuing throughout the war, Illinois mustered 150 infantry regiments, which were numbered from the 7th to the 156th regiments. Seventeen cavalry regiments were also gathered, as well as two light artillery regiments.[[44]](#cite_note-46) The town of [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo,_Illinois "Cairo, Illinois"), at the southern tip of the state at the confluence of the Mississippi and Ohio Rivers, served as a strategically important supply base and training center for the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") army. For several months, both General [Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant") and Admiral [Foote](/wiki/Andrew_Hull_Foote "Andrew Hull Foote") had headquarters in Cairo.

During the Civil War, and more so afterwards, Chicago's population skyrocketed, which increased its prominence. The [Pullman Strike](/wiki/Pullman_Strike "Pullman Strike") and [Haymarket Riot](/wiki/Haymarket_affair "Haymarket affair"), in particular, greatly influenced the development of the American [labor movement](/wiki/Labour_movement "Labour movement"). From Sunday, October 8, 1871, until Tuesday, October 10, 1871, the [Great Chicago Fire](/wiki/Great_Chicago_Fire "Great Chicago Fire") burned in downtown Chicago, killing 300 people and destroying four sq mi (10 km2).[[45]](#cite_note-47)

### 20th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Protestors_and_Chicago_Police_Officers_in_Grant_Park_-_DPLA_-_2972723d78e7f60f542da94846a6f9a6_%281%29_%28cropa%29.jpg/250px-Protestors_and_Chicago_Police_Officers_in_Grant_Park_-_DPLA_-_2972723d78e7f60f542da94846a6f9a6_%281%29_%28cropa%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Protestors_and_Chicago_Police_Officers_in_Grant_Park_-_DPLA_-_2972723d78e7f60f542da94846a6f9a6_(1)_(cropa).jpg)

Police and protesters at the [1968 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/1968_Democratic_National_Convention "1968 Democratic National Convention") in Chicago.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/16/Alton_Illinois_sinking_in_1993.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alton_Illinois_sinking_in_1993.jpg)

Rising waters in [Alton](/wiki/Alton,_Illinois "Alton, Illinois") in 1993.

At the turn of the 20th century, Illinois had a population of nearly 5 million. Many people from other parts of the country were attracted to the state by employment caused by the expanding industrial base. Whites were 98% of the state's population.[[46]](#cite_note-census-48) Bolstered by continued [immigration from southern and eastern Europe](/wiki/History_of_immigration_to_the_United_States "History of immigration to the United States"), and by the African-American [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") from the South, Illinois grew and emerged as one of the most important states in the union. By the end of the century, the population had reached 12.4 million.

The [Century of Progress](/wiki/Century_of_Progress "Century of Progress") [World's fair](/wiki/World%27s_fair "World's fair") was held at Chicago in 1933. Oil strikes in [Marion County](/wiki/Marion_County,_Illinois "Marion County, Illinois") and [Crawford County](/wiki/Crawford_County,_Illinois "Crawford County, Illinois") led to a boom in 1937, and by 1939, Illinois ranked fourth in U.S. oil production. Illinois manufactured 6.1 percent of total United States military armaments produced during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), ranking seventh among the 48 states.[[47]](#cite_note-49) Chicago became an ocean port with the opening of the [Saint Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_Seaway "Saint Lawrence Seaway") in 1959. The seaway and the [Illinois Waterway](/wiki/Illinois_Waterway "Illinois Waterway") connected Chicago to both the Mississippi River and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). In 1960, [Ray Kroc](/wiki/Ray_Kroc "Ray Kroc") opened the first [McDonald's](/wiki/McDonald%27s "McDonald's") franchise in [Des Plaines](/wiki/Des_Plaines,_Illinois "Des Plaines, Illinois"), which was demolished in 1984.[[48]](#cite_note-:0-50) In 1985 a replica was built on the same site to recreate how the original one looked.[[48]](#cite_note-:0-50) Though this replica was demolished in 2017, due to repeated flooding of the building.[[49]](#cite_note-51)[[50]](#cite_note-52)

Illinois had a prominent role in the emergence of the [nuclear age](/wiki/Nuclear_age "Nuclear age"). In 1942, as part of the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project"), the [University of Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_Pile-1 "Chicago Pile-1") conducted the first sustained [nuclear chain reaction](/wiki/Nuclear_chain_reaction "Nuclear chain reaction"). In 1957, [Argonne National Laboratory](/wiki/Argonne_National_Laboratory "Argonne National Laboratory"), near [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), activated the first experimental nuclear power generating system in the United States. By 1960, the first privately financed nuclear plant in the United States, [Dresden 1](/wiki/Dresden_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Dresden Nuclear Power Plant"), was dedicated near [Morris](/wiki/Morris,_Illinois "Morris, Illinois"). In 1967, [Fermilab](/wiki/Fermilab "Fermilab"), a national nuclear research facility near [Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_Illinois "Batavia, Illinois"), opened a [particle accelerator](/wiki/Particle_accelerator "Particle accelerator"), which was the world's largest for over 40 years. With eleven plants currently operating, Illinois leads all states in the amount of electricity generated from nuclear power.[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-54)

In 1961, Illinois became the first state in the nation to adopt the recommendation of the [American Law Institute](/wiki/American_Law_Institute "American Law Institute") and pass a comprehensive criminal code revision that repealed the law against [sodomy](/wiki/Sodomy_laws_in_the_United_States "Sodomy laws in the United States"). The code also abrogated [common law crimes](/wiki/Common_law_crime "Common law crime") and established an [age of consent](/wiki/Age_of_consent "Age of consent") of 18.[[53]](#cite_note-55) The state's [fourth constitution](/wiki/Illinois_Constitution "Illinois Constitution") was adopted in 1970, replacing the 1870 document.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

The first [Farm Aid](/wiki/Farm_Aid "Farm Aid") concert was held in [Champaign](/wiki/Champaign,_Illinois "Champaign, Illinois") to benefit American farmers, in 1985. The worst [upper Mississippi River](/wiki/Upper_Mississippi_River "Upper Mississippi River") flood of the century, the [Great Flood of 1993](/wiki/Great_Flood_of_1993 "Great Flood of 1993"), inundated many towns and thousands of acres of farmland.[[29]](#cite_note-Biles-31)

### 21st century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Jackson_Avenue_at_Federal_Plaza%2C_Stay_Home_City_of_Chicago_Ad.jpg/250px-Jackson_Avenue_at_Federal_Plaza%2C_Stay_Home_City_of_Chicago_Ad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jackson_Avenue_at_Federal_Plaza,_Stay_Home_City_of_Chicago_Ad.jpg)

A COVID-19 safety message in the Chicago Loop

Illinois entered the 21st century under Republican Governor [George Ryan](/wiki/George_Ryan "George Ryan"). Near the end of his term in January 2003, following a string of high-profile exonerations, Ryan commuted all [death sentences](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_Illinois "Capital punishment in Illinois") in the state.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

The [2002 election](/wiki/2002_Illinois_gubernatorial_election "2002 Illinois gubernatorial election") brought Democrat [Rod Blagojevich](/wiki/Rod_Blagojevich "Rod Blagojevich") to the governor's mansion. It also brought future president [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") into a committee leadership position in the Illinois Senate, where he drafted the [Health Care Justice Act](/wiki/Health_Care_Justice_Act "Health Care Justice Act"), a forerunner of the [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act").[[56]](#cite_note-fight-tested-58) Obama's election to the presidency in Blagojevich's second term set off a chain of events culminating in [Blagojevich's impeachment](/wiki/Rod_Blagojevich_corruption_charges "Rod Blagojevich corruption charges"), trial, and subsequent criminal conviction and imprisonment, making Blagojevich the second consecutive Illinois governor to be convicted on federal corruption charges.[[57]](#cite_note-59)

Blagojevich's replacement [Pat Quinn](/wiki/Pat_Quinn_(politician) "Pat Quinn (politician)") was defeated by Republican [Bruce Rauner](/wiki/Bruce_Rauner "Bruce Rauner") in the [2014 election](/wiki/2014_Illinois_gubernatorial_election "2014 Illinois gubernatorial election"). Disagreements between the governor and legislature over budgetary policy led to the [Illinois Budget Impasse](/wiki/Illinois_Budget_Impasse "Illinois Budget Impasse"), a 793-day period stretching from 2015 to 2018 in which the state had no budget and struggled to pay its bills.[[58]](#cite_note-60)

On August 28, 2017, Rauner signed a bill into law that prohibited state and local police from arresting anyone solely due to their immigration status or due to federal detainers.[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62) Some fellow Republicans criticized Rauner for his action, claiming the bill made Illinois a [sanctuary state](/wiki/Sanctuary_city "Sanctuary city").[[61]](#cite_note-63)

In the [2018 election](/wiki/2018_Illinois_gubernatorial_election "2018 Illinois gubernatorial election"), Rauner was replaced by [J. B. Pritzker](/wiki/J._B._Pritzker "J. B. Pritzker"), returning the state government to a Democratic [trifecta](/wiki/Government_trifecta "Government trifecta").[[62]](#cite_note-64) In January 2020 the state [legalized marijuana](/wiki/Illinois_Cannabis_Regulation_and_Tax_Act "Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act").[[63]](#cite_note-65) On March 9, 2020, Pritzker issued a disaster proclamation due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Illinois "COVID-19 pandemic in Illinois"). He ended the state of emergency in May 2023.[[64]](#cite_note-66)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Illinois](/wiki/Geography_of_Illinois "Geography of Illinois")

Further information: [List of ecoregions in Illinois](/wiki/List_of_ecoregions_in_Illinois "List of ecoregions in Illinois")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Illinois_municipalities.svg/250px-Illinois_municipalities.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Illinois_municipalities.svg)

[Illinois municipalities](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Illinois "List of municipalities in Illinois")

Illinois is located in the [Midwest region](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") of the United States and is one of the eight states in the [Great Lakes region](/wiki/Great_Lakes_region_(North_America) "Great Lakes region (North America)") of North America. Illinois's eastern border with [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") consists of a north–south line at 87° 31′ 30″ west longitude in [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") at the north, to the [Wabash River](/wiki/Wabash_River "Wabash River") in the south above [Post Vincennes](/wiki/Vincennes,_Indiana "Vincennes, Indiana"). The Wabash River continues as the eastern/southeastern border with Indiana until the Wabash enters the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River"). This marks the beginning of Illinois's southern border with [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"), which runs along the northern shoreline of the Ohio River.[[65]](#cite_note-67) Most of the western border with [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri") and [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa") is the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"); Kaskaskia is an [exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") of Illinois, lying west of the Mississippi and reachable only from Missouri. The state's northern border with [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") is fixed at 42° 30′ north latitude. The northeastern border of Illinois lies in [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"), within which Illinois shares a water boundary with the state of [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), as well as Wisconsin and Indiana.[[26]](#cite_note-Nelson-28)

### Topography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Looking_eastward_at_Charles_Mound%2C_the_highest_point_in_Illinois.jpg/250px-Looking_eastward_at_Charles_Mound%2C_the_highest_point_in_Illinois.jpg)](/wiki/File:Looking_eastward_at_Charles_Mound,_the_highest_point_in_Illinois.jpg)

[Charles Mound](/wiki/Charles_Mound "Charles Mound"), the highest natural point in Illinois at 1,235 feet (376 m) above sea level, is located in the [Driftless Area](/wiki/Driftless_Area "Driftless Area") in the northwestern part of the state.

Though Illinois lies entirely in the [Interior Plains](/wiki/Interior_Plains "Interior Plains"), it does have some minor variation in its elevation. In extreme northwestern Illinois, the [Driftless Area](/wiki/Driftless_Area "Driftless Area"), a region of unglaciated and therefore higher and more rugged topography, occupies a small part of the state. [Southern Illinois](/wiki/Southern_Illinois "Southern Illinois") includes the hilly areas around the [Shawnee National Forest](/wiki/Shawnee_National_Forest "Shawnee National Forest").

[Charles Mound](/wiki/Charles_Mound "Charles Mound"), located in the Driftless region, has the state's highest natural elevation above sea level at 1,235 ft (376 m). Other highlands include the [Shawnee Hills](/wiki/Shawnee_Hills "Shawnee Hills") in the south, and there is varying topography along its rivers; the [Illinois River](/wiki/Illinois_River "Illinois River") bisects the state northeast to southwest. The floodplain on the Mississippi River from [Alton](/wiki/Alton,_Illinois "Alton, Illinois") to the [Kaskaskia River](/wiki/Kaskaskia_River "Kaskaskia River") is known as the [American Bottom](/wiki/American_Bottom "American Bottom").

### Geology

Main article: [Geology of Illinois](/wiki/Geology_of_Illinois "Geology of Illinois")

During the early part of the [Paleozoic Era](/wiki/Paleozoic_Era "Paleozoic Era"), the area that would one day become Illinois was submerged beneath a shallow sea and located near the Equator. Diverse marine life lived at this time, including [trilobites](/wiki/Trilobite "Trilobite"), [brachiopods](/wiki/Brachiopod "Brachiopod"), and [crinoids](/wiki/Crinoid "Crinoid"). Changing environmental conditions led to the formation of large [coal swamps](/wiki/Coal_forest "Coal forest") in the [Carboniferous](/wiki/Carboniferous "Carboniferous").

Illinois was above sea level for at least part of the [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic"), but by its end was again submerged by the [Western Interior Seaway](/wiki/Western_Interior_Seaway "Western Interior Seaway"). This receded by the [Eocene Epoch](/wiki/Eocene "Eocene").

During the [Pleistocene Epoch](/wiki/Pleistocene_Epoch "Pleistocene Epoch"), vast ice sheets covered much of Illinois, with only the [Driftless Area](/wiki/Driftless_Area "Driftless Area") remaining exposed. These [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") carved the basin of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") and left behind traces of ancient glacial lakes and [moraines](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine").[[66]](#cite_note-68)

### Divisions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Cairo_Ohio_River_Bridge_at_sunset.jpg/250px-Cairo_Ohio_River_Bridge_at_sunset.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cairo_Ohio_River_Bridge_at_sunset.jpg)

At 279 feet (85 m) above sea level,[[67]](#cite_note-69) the lowest elevation point in the state is located near [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo,_Illinois "Cairo, Illinois") and the confluence of the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers.

Illinois has three major geographical divisions. [Northern Illinois](/wiki/Northern_Illinois "Northern Illinois") is dominated by [Chicago metropolitan area](/wiki/Chicago_metropolitan_area "Chicago metropolitan area"), or Chicagoland, which is the city of Chicago and its suburbs, and the adjoining exurban area into which the metropolis is expanding. As defined by the federal government, the Chicago metro area includes several counties in Illinois, [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), and [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), and has a population of over 9.8 million. Chicago itself is a cosmopolitan city, densely populated, industrialized, the transportation hub of the nation, and settled by a wide variety of ethnic groups. The city of [Rockford](/wiki/Rockford,_Illinois "Rockford, Illinois"), Illinois's third-largest city and center of the state's fourth largest metropolitan area, sits along Interstates [39](/wiki/Interstate_39 "Interstate 39") and [90](/wiki/Interstate_90 "Interstate 90") some 75 mi (121 km) northwest of Chicago. The [Quad Cities](/wiki/Quad_Cities "Quad Cities") region, located along the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") in northern Illinois, had a population of 381,342 in 2011.

The midsection of Illinois is the second major division, called [Central Illinois](/wiki/Central_Illinois "Central Illinois"). Historically [prairie](/wiki/Prairie "Prairie"), it is now mainly agricultural and known as the Heart of Illinois. It is characterized by small towns and medium–small cities. The western section (west of the Illinois River) was originally part of the [Military Tract of 1812](/wiki/Military_Tract_of_1812 "Military Tract of 1812") and forms the conspicuous western bulge of the state. Agriculture, particularly [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize") and [soybeans](/wiki/Soybean "Soybean"), as well as educational institutions and manufacturing centers, figure prominently in Central Illinois. Cities include [Peoria](/wiki/Peoria,_Illinois "Peoria, Illinois"); [Springfield](/wiki/Springfield,_Illinois "Springfield, Illinois"), the state capital; [Quincy](/wiki/Quincy,_Illinois "Quincy, Illinois"); [Decatur](/wiki/Decatur,_Illinois "Decatur, Illinois"); [Bloomington-Normal](/wiki/Bloomington-Normal,_Illinois "Bloomington-Normal, Illinois"); and [Champaign](/wiki/Champaign,_Illinois "Champaign, Illinois")-[Urbana](/wiki/Urbana,_Illinois "Urbana, Illinois").[[26]](#cite_note-Nelson-28)

The third division is [Southern Illinois](/wiki/Southern_Illinois "Southern Illinois"), comprising the area south of [U.S. Route 50](/wiki/U.S._Route_50_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 50 in Illinois"), including [Little Egypt](/wiki/Southern_Illinois "Southern Illinois"), near the juncture of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River"). Southern Illinois is the site of the ancient city of [Cahokia](/wiki/Cahokia "Cahokia"), as well as the site of the first state capital at [Kaskaskia](/wiki/Kaskaskia,_Illinois "Kaskaskia, Illinois"), which today is separated from the rest of the state by the Mississippi River.[[26]](#cite_note-Nelson-28)[[68]](#cite_note-Horsley-70) This region has a somewhat warmer winter climate, different variety of crops (including some cotton farming in the past), more rugged topography (due to the area remaining unglaciated during the [Illinoian Stage](/wiki/Illinoian_(stage) "Illinoian (stage)"), unlike most of the rest of the state), as well as small-scale oil deposits and [coal mining](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining"). The Illinois suburbs of [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri"), such as [East St. Louis](/wiki/East_St._Louis "East St. Louis"), are located in this region, and collectively, they are known as the [Metro-East](/wiki/Metro-East "Metro-East"). The other somewhat significant concentration of population in Southern Illinois is the Carbondale-Marion-Herrin, Illinois Combined Statistical Area centered on [Carbondale](/wiki/Carbondale,_Illinois "Carbondale, Illinois") and [Marion](/wiki/Marion,_Illinois "Marion, Illinois"), a two-county area that is home to 123,272 residents.[[26]](#cite_note-Nelson-28) A portion of southeastern Illinois is part of the extended [Evansville, Indiana](/wiki/Evansville,_Indiana "Evansville, Indiana"), Metro Area, locally referred to as the Tri-State with Indiana and Kentucky. Seven Illinois counties are in the area.

In addition to these three, largely latitudinally defined divisions, all of the region outside the Chicago metropolitan area is often called "[downstate](/wiki/Downstate_Illinois "Downstate Illinois")" Illinois. This term is flexible, but is generally meant to mean everything outside the influence of the Chicago area. Thus, some cities in *Northern* Illinois, such as [DeKalb](/wiki/DeKalb,_Illinois "DeKalb, Illinois"), which is west of Chicago, and [Rockford](/wiki/Rockford,_Illinois "Rockford, Illinois")—which is actually north of Chicago—are sometimes incorrectly considered to be 'downstate'.

### Climate

Main articles: [Climate of Illinois](/wiki/Climate_of_Illinois "Climate of Illinois") and [List of Illinois tornadoes](/wiki/List_of_Illinois_tornadoes "List of Illinois tornadoes")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Illinois_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Illinois_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Illinois_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Illinois

Illinois has a climate that varies widely throughout the year. Because of its nearly 400-mile distance between its northernmost and southernmost extremes, as well as its mid-continental situation, most of Illinois has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfa*), with hot, humid summers and cold winters. The southern part of the state, from about [Carbondale](/wiki/Carbondale,_Illinois "Carbondale, Illinois") southward, has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (Koppen *Cfa*), with more moderate winters. Average yearly precipitation for Illinois varies from just over 48 in (1,219 mm) at the southern tip to around 35 in (889 mm) in the northern portion of the state. Normal annual snowfall exceeds 38 in (965 mm) in the Chicago area, while the southern portion of the state normally receives less than 14 in (356 mm).[[69]](#cite_note-Climate_Maps-71) The all-time high temperature was 117 °F (47 °C), recorded on July 14, 1954, at [East St. Louis](/wiki/East_St._Louis,_Illinois "East St. Louis, Illinois"), and the all-time low temperature was −38 °F (−39 °C), recorded on January 31, 2019, during the [January 2019 North American cold wave](/wiki/January_2019_North_American_cold_wave "January 2019 North American cold wave") at a weather station near [Mount Carroll](/wiki/Mount_Carroll,_Illinois "Mount Carroll, Illinois"),[[70]](#cite_note-72)[[71]](#cite_note-SCEC-73) and confirmed on March 5, 2019.[[72]](#cite_note-SCECreport-74) This followed the previous record of −36 °F (−38 °C) recorded on January 5, 1999, near [Congerville](/wiki/Congerville,_Illinois "Congerville, Illinois").[[72]](#cite_note-SCECreport-74) Prior to the Mount Carroll record, a temperature of −37 °F (−38 °C) was recorded on January 15, 2009, at [Rochelle](/wiki/Rochelle,_Illinois "Rochelle, Illinois"), but at a weather station not subjected to the same quality control as official records.[[73]](#cite_note-pjstar-lose-75)[[74]](#cite_note-Weather_Underground-76)

Illinois averages approximately 51 days of [thunderstorm](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm") activity a year, which ranks somewhat above average in the number of thunderstorm days for the United States. Illinois is vulnerable to tornadoes, with an average of 54 occurring annually, which puts much of the state at around five tornadoes per 10,000 sq mi (30,000 km2) annually.[[75]](#cite_note-Annual_average_number_of_tornadoes-77) Some of [Tornado Alley](/wiki/Tornado_Alley "Tornado Alley")'s deadliest tornadoes on record have occurred in the state. The [Tri-State Tornado](/wiki/Tri-State_Tornado "Tri-State Tornado") of 1925 killed 695 people in three states; 613 of the victims died in Illinois.[[76]](#cite_note-paddock_nws-78)

### Urban areas

See also: [Illinois statistical areas](/wiki/Illinois_statistical_areas "Illinois statistical areas") and [List of municipalities in Illinois](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Illinois "List of municipalities in Illinois")

Chicago is the largest city in the state and the [third-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States, with a population of 2,746,388 in 2020. Furthermore, over 7 million residents of the [Chicago metropolitan area](/wiki/Chicago_metropolitan_area "Chicago metropolitan area") reside in Illinois. The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") currently lists seven other cities with populations of over 100,000 within the state. This includes the Chicago [satellite towns](/wiki/Satellite_town "Satellite town") of [Aurora](/wiki/Aurora,_Illinois "Aurora, Illinois"), [Joliet](/wiki/Joliet,_Illinois "Joliet, Illinois"), [Naperville](/wiki/Naperville,_Illinois "Naperville, Illinois"), and [Elgin](/wiki/Elgin,_Illinois "Elgin, Illinois"), as well as the cities of [Rockford](/wiki/Rockford,_Illinois "Rockford, Illinois"), the most populous city in the state outside of the Chicago area; [Springfield](/wiki/Springfield,_Illinois "Springfield, Illinois"), the state's capital; and [Peoria](/wiki/Peoria,_Illinois "Peoria, Illinois").

The most populated city in the state south of Springfield is [Belleville](/wiki/Belleville,_Illinois "Belleville, Illinois"), with 42,000 residents. It is located in the [Metro East](/wiki/Metro_East "Metro East") region of [Greater St. Louis](/wiki/Greater_St._Louis "Greater St. Louis"), the second-most populous urban area in Illinois with over 700,000 residents. Other major urban areas include the [Peoria metropolitan area](/wiki/Peoria,_Illinois,_metropolitan_area "Peoria, Illinois, metropolitan area"), [Rockford metropolitan area](/wiki/Rockford_metropolitan_area,_Illinois "Rockford metropolitan area, Illinois"), [Champaign–Urbana metropolitan area](/wiki/Champaign%E2%80%93Urbana_metropolitan_area "Champaign–Urbana metropolitan area") (home to the [University of Illinois](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_Urbana-Champaign "University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign")), [Springfield metropolitan area](/wiki/Springfield_metropolitan_area,_Illinois "Springfield metropolitan area, Illinois"), the Illinois portion of the [Quad Cities](/wiki/Quad_Cities "Quad Cities") area, and the [Bloomington–Normal metropolitan area](/wiki/Bloomington%E2%80%93Normal "Bloomington–Normal").

## Demographics

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | It has been suggested that this section be [**split**](/wiki/Wikipedia:Splitting "Wikipedia:Splitting") out into another article titled *[Demographics of Illinois](/wiki/Demographics_of_Illinois "Demographics of Illinois")*. ([**Discuss**](/wiki/Talk:Illinois "Talk:Illinois")) *(September 2024)* |

The [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") found that the population of Illinois was 12,812,508 in the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), moving from the fifth-largest state to the sixth-largest state (losing out to [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania")). Illinois' population slightly declined in 2020 from the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") by just over 18,000 residents and the overall population was quite higher than recent census estimates.[[85]](#cite_note-87)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Illinois_2020_Population_Density.png/250px-Illinois_2020_Population_Density.png)](/wiki/File:Illinois_2020_Population_Density.png)

Illinois 2020 Population Density Map

Illinois is the most populous state in the [Midwest region](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"). Chicago, the [third-most populous city in the United States](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population"), is the center of the [Chicago metropolitan area](/wiki/Chicago_metropolitan_area "Chicago metropolitan area") or Chicagoland, as this area is nicknamed. Although the Chicago metropolitan area comprises only 9% of the land area of the state, it contains 65% of the state's residents, with 21.4% of Illinois' population living in the city of Chicago itself as of 2020.[[86]](#cite_note-88) The five [most populous counties in the state](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_Illinois "List of counties in Illinois"), as of 2024, are [Cook](/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois "Cook County, Illinois") (5,182,617), [DuPage](/wiki/DuPage_County,_Illinois "DuPage County, Illinois") (937,142), [Lake](/wiki/Lake_County,_Illinois "Lake County, Illinois") (718,604), [Will](/wiki/Will_County,_Illinois "Will County, Illinois") (708,583), and [Kane](/wiki/Kane_County,_Illinois "Kane County, Illinois") (520,997), all located in the Chicago metropolitan area. While the state's population has declined in recent years, according to the 2020 census, these losses do not arise from the Chicago metro area; rather the declines are from the Downstate counties.[[87]](#cite_note-VinickyWhere2020-89) As of the 2020 census, the state's geographic mean [center of population](/wiki/Center_of_population "Center of population") is located at 41° 18′ 43″N 88° 22 23″W in [Grundy County](/wiki/Grundy_County,_Illinois "Grundy County, Illinois"), about six miles northwest of [Coal City](/wiki/Coal_City,_Illinois "Coal City, Illinois").[[88]](#cite_note-90)

Illinois is the most racially and ethnically diverse state in the Midwestern United States. By several metrics, including racial and ethnic background, religious affiliation, and percentage of rural and urban divide, Illinois is the most representative of the larger [demography of the United States](/wiki/Demography_of_the_United_States "Demography of the United States").[[89]](#cite_note-91)
