### Music

Main article: [Music of Illinois](/wiki/Music_of_Illinois "Music of Illinois")

Illinois is a leader in music education, having hosted the [Midwest Clinic International Band and Orchestra Conference](/wiki/Midwest_Clinic "Midwest Clinic") since 1946, as well being home to the Illinois Music Educators Association (ILMEA, formerly IMEA), one of the largest professional music educator's organizations in the country. Each summer since 2004, [Southern Illinois University Carbondale](/wiki/Southern_Illinois_University_Carbondale "Southern Illinois University Carbondale") has played host to the Southern Illinois Music Festival, which presents dozens of performances throughout the region. Past featured artists include the [Eroica Trio](/wiki/Eroica_Trio "Eroica Trio") and violinist [David Kim](/wiki/David_Kim_(violinist) "David Kim (violinist)").

[Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), in the northeast corner of the state, is a [major center for music](/wiki/Music_of_Chicago "Music of Chicago")[[254]](#cite_note-259) in the [midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") where distinctive forms of blues (greatly responsible for the future creation of [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll")), and [house music](/wiki/House_music "House music"), a genre of electronic dance music, were developed.

The [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") of poor black workers from the South into the industrial cities brought traditional [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") and [blues music](/wiki/Blues_music "Blues music") to the city, resulting in [Chicago blues](/wiki/Chicago_blues "Chicago blues") and "Chicago-style" [Dixieland jazz](/wiki/Dixieland_jazz "Dixieland jazz"). Notable blues artists included [Muddy Waters](/wiki/Muddy_Waters "Muddy Waters"), [Junior Wells](/wiki/Junior_Wells "Junior Wells"), [Howlin' Wolf](/wiki/Howlin%27_Wolf "Howlin' Wolf") and both [Sonny Boy Williamsons](/wiki/Sonny_Boy_Williamson_II "Sonny Boy Williamson II"); jazz greats included [Nat King Cole](/wiki/Nat_King_Cole "Nat King Cole"), [Gene Ammons](/wiki/Gene_Ammons "Gene Ammons"), [Benny Goodman](/wiki/Benny_Goodman "Benny Goodman"), and [Bud Freeman](/wiki/Bud_Freeman "Bud Freeman"). Chicago is also well known for its [soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music").

In the early 1930s, [Gospel music](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music") began to gain popularity in Chicago due to [Thomas A. Dorsey](/wiki/Thomas_A._Dorsey "Thomas A. Dorsey")'s contributions at [Pilgrim Baptist Church](/wiki/Pilgrim_Baptist_Church "Pilgrim Baptist Church").

In the 1980s and 1990s, [heavy rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock"), [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock"), and [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") also became popular in Chicago. [Orchestras](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra") in Chicago include the [Chicago Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Chicago_Symphony_Orchestra "Chicago Symphony Orchestra"), the [Lyric Opera of Chicago](/wiki/Lyric_Opera_of_Chicago "Lyric Opera of Chicago"), and the [Chicago Sinfonietta](/wiki/Chicago_Sinfonietta "Chicago Sinfonietta").[[255]](#cite_note-260)

### Movies

[John Hughes](/wiki/John_Hughes_(filmmaker) "John Hughes (filmmaker)"), who moved from [Grosse Pointe](/wiki/Grosse_Pointe,_Michigan "Grosse Pointe, Michigan") to [Northbrook](/wiki/Northbrook,_Illinois "Northbrook, Illinois"), based many films of his in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago,_Illinois "Chicago, Illinois"), and its suburbs. [Ferris Bueller's Day Off](/wiki/Ferris_Bueller%27s_Day_Off "Ferris Bueller's Day Off"), [Home Alone](/wiki/Home_Alone "Home Alone"), [The Breakfast Club](/wiki/The_Breakfast_Club "The Breakfast Club"), and all his films take place in the fictional Shermer, Illinois (the original name of Northbrook was Shermerville, and Hughes's High School, [Glenbrook North High School](/wiki/Glenbrook_North_High_School "Glenbrook North High School"), is on Shermer Road). Most locations in his films include Glenbrook North, the former [Maine North High School](/wiki/Maine_North_High_School "Maine North High School"), the [Ben Rose House](/wiki/Ben_Rose_House "Ben Rose House") in Highland Park, and the famous Home Alone house in [Winnetka, Illinois](/wiki/Winnetka,_Illinois "Winnetka, Illinois").

### Recreation

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of protected areas of Illinois](/wiki/List_of_protected_areas_of_Illinois "List of protected areas of Illinois").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Garden_of_the_Gods_Sunset.jpg/250px-Garden_of_the_Gods_Sunset.jpg)](/wiki/File:Garden_of_the_Gods_Sunset.jpg)

Sunset on the [Garden of the Gods Wilderness](/wiki/Garden_of_the_Gods_Wilderness "Garden of the Gods Wilderness"), part of the [Shawnee National Forest](/wiki/Shawnee_National_Forest "Shawnee National Forest") in southern Illinois.

The [Illinois state parks](/wiki/Illinois_state_parks "Illinois state parks") system began in 1908 with what is now [Fort Massac](/wiki/Fort_Massac "Fort Massac") State Park, becoming the first park in a system encompassing more than 60 parks and about the same number of recreational and wildlife areas.

Areas under the protection of the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") include: the [Illinois and Michigan Canal National Heritage Corridor](/wiki/Illinois_and_Michigan_Canal "Illinois and Michigan Canal") near [Lockport](/wiki/Lockport,_Illinois "Lockport, Illinois"),[[256]](#cite_note-261) the [Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_National_Historic_Trail "Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail"), the [Lincoln Home National Historic Site](/wiki/Lincoln_Home_National_Historic_Site "Lincoln Home National Historic Site") in Springfield, the [Mormon Pioneer National Historic Trail](/wiki/Mormon_Trail "Mormon Trail"), the [Trail of Tears National Historic Trail](/wiki/Trail_of_Tears "Trail of Tears"), the [American Discovery Trail](/wiki/American_Discovery_Trail "American Discovery Trail"),[[257]](#cite_note-262) the [Pullman National Monument](/wiki/Pullman_National_Monument "Pullman National Monument"), and [New Philadelphia Town Site](/wiki/New_Philadelphia_Town_Site "New Philadelphia Town Site"). The federal government also manages the [Shawnee National Forest](/wiki/Shawnee_National_Forest "Shawnee National Forest") and the [Midewin National Tallgrass Prairie](/wiki/Midewin_National_Tallgrass_Prairie "Midewin National Tallgrass Prairie").

### Sports

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of professional sports teams in Illinois](/wiki/List_of_professional_sports_teams_in_Illinois "List of professional sports teams in Illinois").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Soldier_Field_during_Chicago_Bears_home_game_against_the_San_Francisco_49ers_on_October_29%2C_2006.jpg/250px-Soldier_Field_during_Chicago_Bears_home_game_against_the_San_Francisco_49ers_on_October_29%2C_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soldier_Field_during_Chicago_Bears_home_game_against_the_San_Francisco_49ers_on_October_29,_2006.jpg)

[Soldier Field](/wiki/Soldier_Field "Soldier Field") is home to the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League")'s [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Wrigley_field_720.jpg/250px-Wrigley_field_720.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wrigley_field_720.jpg)

[Wrigley Field](/wiki/Wrigley_Field "Wrigley Field") is home to [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")'s [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs")

As one of the United States' major metropolises, all [major sports leagues](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada") have teams headquartered in Chicago.

Two [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") teams are located in the state. The [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") play in the second-oldest major league stadium, [Wrigley Field](/wiki/Wrigley_Field "Wrigley Field"), and went the longest length of time without a championship in all of major American sport, from 1908 to 2016, when they won the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series").[[258]](#cite_note-263)[[259]](#cite_note-264) The [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") of the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") won the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") in 2005, their first since 1917. They play on the city's south side at [Rate Field](/wiki/Rate_Field "Rate Field"). The [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears") football team has won nine total [NFL Championships](/wiki/List_of_NFL_champions "List of NFL champions"), the last occurring in [Super Bowl XX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XX "Super Bowl XX") on January 26, 1986. The [Chicago Bulls](/wiki/Chicago_Bulls "Chicago Bulls") of the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") is one of the most recognized [basketball teams](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") in the world, largely as a result of the efforts of [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan"), who led the team to six NBA championships in eight seasons in the 1990s. The [Chicago Blackhawks](/wiki/Chicago_Blackhawks "Chicago Blackhawks") of the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") began playing in [1926](/wiki/1926%E2%80%9327_NHL_season "1926–27 NHL season") and became a member of the [Original Six](/wiki/Original_Six "Original Six") once the NHL dropped to that number of teams during World War II. The Blackhawks have won six [Stanley Cups](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup"), most recently in [2015](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_NHL_season "2014–15 NHL season"). [Chicago Fire FC](/wiki/Chicago_Fire_FC "Chicago Fire FC") is a member of [MLS](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") and has been one of the league's most successful and best-supported clubs since its founding in 1997, winning one league and four [Lamar Hunt U.S. Open Cups](/wiki/Lamar_Hunt_U.S._Open_Cup "Lamar Hunt U.S. Open Cup") in that timespan. The team played in [Bridgeview](/wiki/Bridgeview,_Illinois "Bridgeview, Illinois"), adjacent to Chicago from 2006 to 2019. The team now plays at Soldier Field in Chicago.

The [Chicago Red Stars](/wiki/Chicago_Red_Stars "Chicago Red Stars") have played at the top level of U.S. women's soccer since their formation in 2009, except in the 2011 season. The team currently plays in the [National Women's Soccer League](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Soccer_League "National Women's Soccer League"), playing at [SeatGeek Stadium](/wiki/SeatGeek_Stadium "SeatGeek Stadium"), the Bridgeview venue it formerly shared with Fire FC. The [Chicago Sky](/wiki/Chicago_Sky "Chicago Sky") have played in the [Women's National Basketball Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association") (WNBA) since 2006. The Sky won their first WNBA Championship in 2021. They play at [Wintrust Arena](/wiki/Wintrust_Arena "Wintrust Arena") in Chicago. The [Chicago Bandits](/wiki/Chicago_Bandits "Chicago Bandits") of the [NPF](/wiki/National_Pro_Fastpitch "National Pro Fastpitch"), a women's softball league, have won four league titles, most recently in 2016. They play at [Parkway Bank Sports Complex](/wiki/Parkway_Bank_Sports_Complex "Parkway Bank Sports Complex") in [Rosemont, Illinois](/wiki/Rosemont,_Illinois "Rosemont, Illinois"), in the Chicago area.

Many [minor league](/wiki/Minor_league "Minor league") teams also call Illinois their home. They include the [Bloomington Edge](/wiki/Bloomington_Edge "Bloomington Edge") of the [Indoor Football League](/wiki/Indoor_Football_League "Indoor Football League"), [Bloomington Flex](/wiki/Bloomington_Flex "Bloomington Flex") of the [Midwest Professional Basketball Association](/wiki/Midwest_Professional_Basketball_Association "Midwest Professional Basketball Association"), [Chicago Dogs](/wiki/Chicago_Dogs "Chicago Dogs") of the [American Association of Professional Baseball](/wiki/American_Association_of_Professional_Baseball "American Association of Professional Baseball"), [Chicago Fire FC II](/wiki/Chicago_Fire_FC_II "Chicago Fire FC II") of [MLS Next Pro](/wiki/MLS_Next_Pro "MLS Next Pro"), [Chicago Wolves](/wiki/Chicago_Wolves "Chicago Wolves") of the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League"), [Gateway Grizzlies](/wiki/Gateway_Grizzlies "Gateway Grizzlies") of the [Frontier League](/wiki/Frontier_League "Frontier League"), [Kane County Cougars](/wiki/Kane_County_Cougars "Kane County Cougars") of the [American Association](/wiki/American_Association_of_Professional_Baseball "American Association of Professional Baseball"), [Joliet Slammers](/wiki/Joliet_Slammers "Joliet Slammers") of the Frontier League, [Peoria Chiefs](/wiki/Peoria_Chiefs "Peoria Chiefs") of the [Midwest League](/wiki/Midwest_League "Midwest League"), [Peoria Rivermen](/wiki/Peoria_Rivermen_(SPHL) "Peoria Rivermen (SPHL)") of the [SPHL](/wiki/Southern_Professional_Hockey_League "Southern Professional Hockey League"), [Rockford Aviators](/wiki/Rockford_Aviators "Rockford Aviators") of the Frontier League, [Rockford IceHogs](/wiki/Rockford_IceHogs "Rockford IceHogs") of the AHL, [Schaumburg Boomers](/wiki/Schaumburg_Boomers "Schaumburg Boomers") of the Frontier League, [Southern Illinois Miners](/wiki/Southern_Illinois_Miners "Southern Illinois Miners") in the Frontier League, [Windy City Bulls](/wiki/Windy_City_Bulls "Windy City Bulls") of the [NBA G League](/wiki/NBA_G_League "NBA G League"), and [Windy City ThunderBolts](/wiki/Windy_City_ThunderBolts "Windy City ThunderBolts") of the Frontier League.

The state features 13 athletic programs that compete in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I"), the highest level of U.S. college sports. The two most prominent are the [Illinois Fighting Illini](/wiki/Illinois_Fighting_Illini "Illinois Fighting Illini") and [Northwestern Wildcats](/wiki/Northwestern_Wildcats "Northwestern Wildcats"), both members of the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference") and the only ones competing in the "[Power Five conferences](/wiki/Power_Five_conferences "Power Five conferences")". The Fighting Illini football team has won five national championships and three [Rose Bowl Games](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_Game "Rose Bowl Game"), whereas the men's basketball team has won 17 conference seasons and played five Final Fours. Meanwhile, the Wildcats have won eight football conference championships and one Rose Bowl Game. The [Northern Illinois Huskies](/wiki/Northern_Illinois_Huskies "Northern Illinois Huskies") compete in the [Mid-American Conference](/wiki/Mid-American_Conference "Mid-American Conference"), having won four conference championships and earning a bid in the [Orange Bowl](/wiki/Orange_Bowl "Orange Bowl"). Four schools have football programs that compete in the second level of Division I football, the [Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/Football_Championship_Subdivision "Football Championship Subdivision"). The [Illinois State Redbirds](/wiki/Illinois_State_Redbirds "Illinois State Redbirds") and [Southern Illinois Salukis](/wiki/Southern_Illinois_Salukis "Southern Illinois Salukis") are members of the [Missouri Valley Conference](/wiki/Missouri_Valley_Conference "Missouri Valley Conference") for non-football sports and the [Missouri Valley Football Conference](/wiki/Missouri_Valley_Football_Conference "Missouri Valley Football Conference"). The [Eastern Illinois Panthers](/wiki/Eastern_Illinois_Panthers "Eastern Illinois Panthers") and [Western Illinois Leathernecks](/wiki/Western_Illinois_Leathernecks "Western Illinois Leathernecks") are members of the [Ohio Valley Conference](/wiki/Ohio_Valley_Conference "Ohio Valley Conference"). The city of Chicago is home to four Division I programs that do not sponsor football: the [DePaul Blue Demons](/wiki/DePaul_Blue_Demons "DePaul Blue Demons") of the [Big East Conference](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference"), [Loyola Ramblers](/wiki/Loyola_Ramblers "Loyola Ramblers") of the [Atlantic 10 Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_10_Conference "Atlantic 10 Conference"), [UIC Flames](/wiki/UIC_Flames "UIC Flames") of the Missouri Valley Conference, and [Chicago State Cougars](/wiki/Chicago_State_Cougars "Chicago State Cougars") of the [Northeast Conference](/wiki/Northeast_Conference "Northeast Conference"). Finally, two non-football Division I programs are located downstate. The [Bradley Braves](/wiki/Bradley_Braves "Bradley Braves") are Missouri Valley Conference members, and the [SIU Edwardsville Cougars](/wiki/SIU_Edwardsville_Cougars "SIU Edwardsville Cougars") compete in the Ohio Valley Conference.

[Motor racing](/wiki/Motor_racing "Motor racing") oval tracks at the [Chicagoland Speedway](/wiki/Chicagoland_Speedway "Chicagoland Speedway") in [Joliet](/wiki/Joliet,_Illinois "Joliet, Illinois"), the [Chicago Motor Speedway](/wiki/Chicago_Motor_Speedway "Chicago Motor Speedway") in [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero,_Illinois "Cicero, Illinois") and the [Gateway Motorsports Park](/wiki/Gateway_Motorsports_Park "Gateway Motorsports Park") in [Madison](/wiki/Madison,_Illinois "Madison, Illinois"), near St. Louis, have hosted [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR"), [CART](/wiki/Champ_Car "Champ Car"), and [IRL](/wiki/Indy_Racing_League "Indy Racing League") races, whereas the [Sports Car Club of America](/wiki/Sports_Car_Club_of_America "Sports Car Club of America"), among other national and regional [road racing](/wiki/Road_racing "Road racing") clubs, have visited the [Autobahn Country Club](/wiki/Autobahn_Country_Club "Autobahn Country Club") in Joliet, the [Blackhawk Farms Raceway](/wiki/Blackhawk_Farms_Raceway "Blackhawk Farms Raceway") in [South Beloit](/wiki/South_Beloit,_Illinois "South Beloit, Illinois") and the former [Meadowdale International Raceway](/wiki/Meadowdale_International_Raceway "Meadowdale International Raceway") in [Carpentersville](/wiki/Carpentersville,_Illinois "Carpentersville, Illinois"). Illinois also has several [short tracks](/wiki/Short_track_motor_racing "Short track motor racing") and [dragstrips](/wiki/Dragstrip "Dragstrip"). The dragstrip at Gateway International Raceway and the [Route 66 Raceway](/wiki/Route_66_Raceway "Route 66 Raceway"), which sits on the same property as the Chicagoland Speedway, both host [NHRA](/wiki/NHRA "NHRA") drag races.

Illinois features several golf courses, such as [Olympia Fields](/wiki/Olympia_Fields_Country_Club "Olympia Fields Country Club"), [Medinah](/wiki/Medinah_Country_Club "Medinah Country Club"), [Midlothian](/wiki/Midlothian_Country_Club "Midlothian Country Club"), [Cog Hill](/wiki/Cog_Hill_Golf_%26_Country_Club "Cog Hill Golf & Country Club"), and [Conway Farms](/wiki/Conway_Farms_Golf_Club "Conway Farms Golf Club"), which have often hosted the [BMW Championship](/wiki/BMW_Championship_(PGA_Tour) "BMW Championship (PGA Tour)"), [Western Open](/wiki/Western_Open "Western Open"), and [Women's Western Open](/wiki/Women%27s_Western_Open "Women's Western Open"). Also, the state has hosted 13 editions of the [U.S. Open](/wiki/U.S._Open_(golf) "U.S. Open (golf)") (latest at Olympia Fields in 2003), six editions of the [PGA Championship](/wiki/PGA_Championship "PGA Championship") (latest at Medinah in 2006), three editions of the [U.S. Women's Open](/wiki/U.S._Women%27s_Open "U.S. Women's Open") (latest at The Merit Club), the [2009 Solheim Cup](/wiki/2009_Solheim_Cup "2009 Solheim Cup") (at Rich Harvest Farms), and the [2012 Ryder Cup](/wiki/2012_Ryder_Cup "2012 Ryder Cup") (at Medinah). The [John Deere Classic](/wiki/John_Deere_Classic "John Deere Classic") is a regular [PGA Tour](/wiki/PGA_Tour "PGA Tour") event played in the Quad Cities since 1971, whereas the [Encompass Championship](/wiki/Encompass_Championship "Encompass Championship") is a [Champions Tour](/wiki/Champions_Tour "Champions Tour") event since 2013. Previously, the [LPGA State Farm Classic](/wiki/LPGA_State_Farm_Classic "LPGA State Farm Classic") was an [LPGA Tour](/wiki/LPGA_Tour "LPGA Tour") event from 1976 to 2011.

## Law and politics

Main articles: [Law of Illinois](/wiki/Law_of_Illinois "Law of Illinois") and [Politics of Illinois](/wiki/Politics_of_Illinois "Politics of Illinois")

In a 2020 study, Illinois was ranked as the 4th easiest state for citizens to vote in.[[260]](#cite_note-265)

### State government

Main article: [Government of Illinois](/wiki/Government_of_Illinois "Government of Illinois")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Gfp-illinois-springfield-capitol-and-sky.jpg/250px-Gfp-illinois-springfield-capitol-and-sky.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gfp-illinois-springfield-capitol-and-sky.jpg)

The [Illinois State Capitol](/wiki/Illinois_State_Capitol "Illinois State Capitol") in [Springfield](/wiki/Springfield,_Illinois "Springfield, Illinois")

The [government of Illinois](/wiki/Government_of_Illinois "Government of Illinois"), under the [Constitution of Illinois](/wiki/Constitution_of_Illinois "Constitution of Illinois"), has three branches of government: executive, legislative, and judicial. The executive branch is split into several statewide elected offices, with the governor as chief executive. Legislative functions are granted to the Illinois General Assembly. The judiciary is composed of the Supreme Court and lower courts.

The executive branch is composed of six elected officers and their offices as well as numerous other departments.[[261]](#cite_note-Lind2012ch5-266) The six elected officers are:[[261]](#cite_note-Lind2012ch5-266) [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Illinois "Governor of Illinois"), [Lieutenant Governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Illinois "Lieutenant Governor of Illinois"), [Attorney General](/wiki/Illinois_Attorney_General "Illinois Attorney General"), [Secretary of State](/wiki/Illinois_Secretary_of_State "Illinois Secretary of State"), [Comptroller](/wiki/Illinois_Comptroller "Illinois Comptroller"), and [Treasurer](/wiki/Illinois_Treasurer "Illinois Treasurer"). The government of Illinois has numerous departments, agencies, boards and commissions, but the so-called code departments provide most of the state's services.[[261]](#cite_note-Lind2012ch5-266)[[262]](#cite_note-267)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Illinois_House_of_Representatives.jpg/250px-Illinois_House_of_Representatives.jpg)](/wiki/File:Illinois_House_of_Representatives.jpg)

[Illinois House of Representatives](/wiki/Illinois_House_of_Representatives "Illinois House of Representatives")

The [Illinois General Assembly](/wiki/Illinois_General_Assembly "Illinois General Assembly") is the state legislature, composed of the 118-member [Illinois House of Representatives](/wiki/Illinois_House_of_Representatives "Illinois House of Representatives") and the 59-member [Illinois Senate](/wiki/Illinois_Senate "Illinois Senate"). The members of the General Assembly are elected at the beginning of each even-numbered year. The *[Illinois Compiled Statutes](/wiki/Illinois_Compiled_Statutes "Illinois Compiled Statutes")* (ILCS) are the [codified](/wiki/Codification_(law) "Codification (law)") statutes of a general and permanent nature.[[263]](#cite_note-survey-268)[[264]](#cite_note-tapping-269)

The [Judiciary of Illinois](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Illinois "Judiciary of Illinois") is the unified court system of Illinois. It consists of the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Illinois "Supreme Court of Illinois"), [Appellate Court](/wiki/Illinois_Appellate_Court "Illinois Appellate Court"), and [Circuit Courts](/wiki/Illinois_Circuit_Courts "Illinois Circuit Courts"). The Supreme Court oversees the administration of the court system.

The [administrative divisions of Illinois](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Illinois "Administrative divisions of Illinois") are counties, townships, precincts, cities, towns, villages, and special-purpose districts.[[265]](#cite_note-270) The basic subdivision of Illinois are the 102 counties.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECensus200789-271) Eighty-five of the 102 counties are in turn divided into townships and precincts.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECensus200789-271)[[267]](#cite_note-272) Municipal governments are the cities, villages, and incorporated towns.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECensus200789-271) Some localities possess [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule"), which allows them to govern themselves to a certain extent.[[268]](#cite_note-273)

### Party balance

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Governor_JB_Pritzker_official_portrait_2019_%28crop%29.jpg/250px-Governor_JB_Pritzker_official_portrait_2019_%28crop%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Governor_JB_Pritzker_official_portrait_2019_(crop).jpg)

Governor [J. B. Pritzker](/wiki/J._B._Pritzker "J. B. Pritzker") (D)

In modern national and state politics, Illinois is a [Democratic stronghold](/wiki/Red_states_and_blue_states "Red states and blue states").[[269]](#cite_note-274) Historically, Illinois was a political [swing state](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state"), with near-parity existing between the [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") and the [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") parties. However, in recent elections, the Democratic Party has gained ground, and Illinois has come to be seen as a solid ["blue" state](/wiki/Red_states_and_blue_states "Red states and blue states") in both presidential and congressional campaigns.[[270]](#cite_note-275)[[271]](#cite_note-276) Illinois's Democratic tendencies are mostly attributable to [Cook County](/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois "Cook County, Illinois") and [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), by far the state's largest county and city, respectively, which have long been strongly Democratic. The [collar counties](/wiki/Collar_counties "Collar counties"), affluent suburban counties that surround Cook County, historically voted Republican and helped keep the state competitive; however, they have swung toward the left in recent elections as the national Republican Party has become increasingly conservative, which has cemented Democratic dominance in state politics.[[272]](#cite_note-277) [Outside of the Chicago metropolitan area](/wiki/Downstate_Illinois "Downstate Illinois"), the state's rural areas are heavily Republican. The dominance of the Chicago area in state elections is so overwhelming that it has influenced a [secessionist movement](/wiki/Separation_referendums_in_Illinois "Separation referendums in Illinois") in the downstate region.[[273]](#cite_note-278)

Illinois was long seen as a national bellwether,[[274]](#cite_note-279) supporting the winner in every election in the 20th century, except for [1916](/wiki/1916_United_States_presidential_election_in_Illinois "1916 United States presidential election in Illinois") and [1976](/wiki/1976_United_States_presidential_election_in_Illinois "1976 United States presidential election in Illinois"). Since the [1992 election](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election "1992 United States presidential election"), however, Illinois has trended more toward the Democratic Party and is part of the "[blue wall](/wiki/Blue_wall_(U.S._politics) "Blue wall (U.S. politics)")" of states that have consistently voted Democratic in the last six presidential elections. In [2000](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election_in_Illinois "2000 United States presidential election in Illinois"), [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") became the first Republican to win the presidency without carrying either Illinois or [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"), with [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") repeating the feat in 2016. Illinois has not elected a Republican to the [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") since [Mark Kirk](/wiki/Mark_Kirk "Mark Kirk") won in [2010](/wiki/2010_United_States_Senate_elections_in_Illinois "2010 United States Senate elections in Illinois"); the last Republicans to hold statewide office were Governor [Bruce Rauner](/wiki/Bruce_Rauner "Bruce Rauner") and Lieutenant Governor [Evelyn Sanguinetti](/wiki/Evelyn_Sanguinetti "Evelyn Sanguinetti"), who both left office in 2019.

### History of corruption

Main article: [Political corruption in Illinois](/wiki/Political_corruption_in_Illinois "Political corruption in Illinois")

Politics in the state have been infamous for highly visible corruption cases, as well as for crusading reformers, such as governors [Adlai Stevenson](/wiki/Adlai_Stevenson_II "Adlai Stevenson II") and [James R. Thompson](/wiki/James_R._Thompson "James R. Thompson"). In 2006, former governor [George Ryan](/wiki/George_Ryan "George Ryan") was convicted of racketeering and bribery, leading to a six-and-a-half-year prison sentence. On December 7, 2011, former governor [Rod Blagojevich](/wiki/Rod_Blagojevich "Rod Blagojevich") was sentenced to 14 years in prison for allegations that he conspired to sell the vacated Senate seat left by President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") to the highest bidder. Blagojevich had earlier been impeached and convicted by the legislature, resulting in his removal from office. In the late 20th century, Congressman [Dan Rostenkowski](/wiki/Dan_Rostenkowski "Dan Rostenkowski") was imprisoned for mail fraud; former governor and federal judge [Otto Kerner Jr.](/wiki/Otto_Kerner_Jr. "Otto Kerner Jr.") was imprisoned for bribery; Secretary of State [Paul Powell](/wiki/Paul_Powell_(politician) "Paul Powell (politician)") was investigated and found to have gained great wealth through bribes, and State Auditor of Public Accounts (Comptroller) [Orville Hodge](/wiki/Orville_Hodge "Orville Hodge") was imprisoned for embezzlement. In 1912, William Lorimer, the GOP boss of Chicago, was expelled from the U.S. Senate for bribery, and in 1921, Governor [Len Small](/wiki/Len_Small "Len Small") was found to have defrauded the state of a million dollars.[[29]](#cite_note-Biles-31)[[68]](#cite_note-Horsley-70)[[275]](#cite_note-280)

### U.S. presidential elections

Main article: [United States presidential elections in Illinois](/wiki/United_States_presidential_elections_in_Illinois "United States presidential elections in Illinois")

Illinois has shown a strong presence in presidential elections. Three presidents have claimed Illinois as their political base when running for president: [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"), [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant"), and most recently [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"). Lincoln was born in [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"), but he moved to Illinois at age 21. He served in the [General Assembly](/wiki/Illinois_General_Assembly "Illinois General Assembly") and represented the [7th congressional district](/wiki/Illinois%27s_7th_congressional_district "Illinois's 7th congressional district") in the [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") before his election to the presidency in 1860. [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant") was born in [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") and had a military career that precluded settling down, but on the eve of the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") and approaching middle age, he moved to Illinois and thus utilized the state as his home and political base when running for president. [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") was born in [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") and made Illinois his home after graduating from [law school](/wiki/Law_school "Law school"), and later represented Illinois in the [U.S. Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). He then became president in 2008, running as a candidate from his Illinois base.

[Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") was born in Illinois, in the city of [Tampico](/wiki/Tampico,_Illinois "Tampico, Illinois"), raised in [Dixon, Illinois](/wiki/Dixon,_Illinois "Dixon, Illinois"), and educated at [Eureka College](/wiki/Eureka_College "Eureka College"), outside [Peoria](/wiki/Peoria,_Illinois "Peoria, Illinois"). Reagan later moved to [California](/wiki/California "California") during his young adulthood. He then became an actor, and later became [California's Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_California "Governor of California") before being elected president.

[Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") was born and raised in the suburbs of Chicago and became the first woman to represent a major political party in the general election of the U.S. presidency. Clinton ran from a platform based in [New York State](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)").

### African-American U.S. senators

[Twelve African-Americans](/wiki/List_of_African-American_United_States_Senators "List of African-American United States Senators") have served as members of the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). Of which three have represented Illinois, the most of any single state: [Carol Moseley-Braun](/wiki/Carol_Moseley_Braun "Carol Moseley Braun"),[[129]](#cite_note-auto11-133) [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"),[[276]](#cite_note-281) and [Roland Burris](/wiki/Roland_Burris "Roland Burris"), who was appointed to replace Obama after his election to the presidency. Moseley-Braun was the first African-American woman to become a U.S. Senator.

### Political families

Several families from Illinois have played particularly prominent roles in politics, in both the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") earlier in the state's history but more recently the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), gaining both statewide and national fame.

#### Ingersoll

The [Ingersoll family of Illinois](/wiki/List_of_United_States_political_families_(I)#The_Ingersolls_of_Illinois "List of United States political families (I)") comprised a pair of brothers who held several prominent elected positions representing Illinois.

* [Ebon C. Ingersoll](/wiki/Ebon_C._Ingersoll "Ebon C. Ingersoll") (1831–1879), Illinois State Representative 1856, U.S. Representative from Illinois 1864–71. Brother of Robert G. Ingersoll.[[277]](#cite_note-auto4-282)
* [Robert G. Ingersoll](/wiki/Robert_G._Ingersoll "Robert G. Ingersoll") (1833–1899), Illinois State Representative 1860, Attorney General of Illinois 1867–69, delegate to the Republican National Convention 1876. Brother of Ebon C. Ingersoll.[[277]](#cite_note-auto4-282)
  + John C. Ingersoll (1860–1903), U.S. Consul in [Cartagena, Colombia](/wiki/Cartagena,_Colombia "Cartagena, Colombia") 1902. Son of Ebon C. Ingersoll.[[277]](#cite_note-auto4-282)

#### Stevenson

The [Stevenson family](/wiki/Stevenson_family "Stevenson family"), initially rooted in central Illinois and later based in the Chicago metropolitan area, has provided four generations of Illinois officeholders.

* [Adlai Stevenson I](/wiki/Adlai_Stevenson_I "Adlai Stevenson I") (1835–1914) was a Vice President of the United States, as well as a [Congressman](/wiki/Congressman "Congressman")
* [Lewis Stevenson](/wiki/Lewis_Stevenson_(politician) "Lewis Stevenson (politician)") (1868–1929), son of Adlai, served as [Illinois Secretary of State](/wiki/Illinois_Secretary_of_State "Illinois Secretary of State").
* [Adlai Stevenson II](/wiki/Adlai_Stevenson_II "Adlai Stevenson II") (1900–1965), son of Lewis, served as [Governor of Illinois](/wiki/Governor_of_Illinois "Governor of Illinois") and as the U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations; he was also the Democratic party's presidential nominee in [1952](/wiki/1952_United_States_presidential_election "1952 United States presidential election") and [1956](/wiki/1956_United_States_presidential_election "1956 United States presidential election"), losing both elections to [Dwight Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_Eisenhower "Dwight Eisenhower").
* [Adlai Stevenson III](/wiki/Adlai_Stevenson_III "Adlai Stevenson III") (1930–2021), son of Adlai II, served ten years as a [United States Senator](/wiki/United_States_Senator "United States Senator").

#### Daley

The [Daley family](/wiki/Daley_family "Daley family")'s powerbase was in Chicago.

* [Richard J. Daley](/wiki/Richard_J._Daley "Richard J. Daley") (1902–1976) served as [Mayor of Chicago](/wiki/Mayor_of_Chicago "Mayor of Chicago") from 1955 to his death.
* [Richard M. Daley](/wiki/Richard_M._Daley "Richard M. Daley") (born 1942), son of Richard J, was Chicago's longest-serving mayor, in office from 1989 to 2011.
* [William M. Daley](/wiki/William_M._Daley "William M. Daley") (born 1948), another son of Richard J, is a former [White House Chief of Staff](/wiki/White_House_Chief_of_Staff "White House Chief of Staff") and has served in a variety of appointed positions.

#### Pritzker

The [Pritzker family](/wiki/Pritzker_family "Pritzker family") is based in Chicago and have played important roles in both the private and the public sectors.

* [Jay Pritzker](/wiki/Jay_Pritzker "Jay Pritzker") (1922–1999), co-founder of [Hyatt Hotel](/wiki/Hyatt_Hotel "Hyatt Hotel") based in Chicago.
* [Penny Pritzker](/wiki/Penny_Pritzker "Penny Pritzker") (born 1959), 38th [United States Secretary of Commerce](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Commerce "United States Secretary of Commerce") under President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama").
* [J. B. Pritzker](/wiki/J._B._Pritzker "J. B. Pritzker") (born 1965), current and 43rd [governor of Illinois](/wiki/Governor_of_Illinois "Governor of Illinois") and co-founder of the Pritzker Group.

#### Madigan

Members of the [Madigan family](/wiki/Mike_Madigan#The_Madigan_family_and_their_role_in_Illinois_government "Mike Madigan") have held extensive influence in Illinois politics.

* [Michael Madigan](/wiki/Mike_Madigan "Mike Madigan") (born 1942), longtime speaker of the [Illinois House of Representatives](/wiki/Illinois_House_of_Representatives "Illinois House of Representatives") and notorious [political boss](/wiki/Political_boss "Political boss").
* [Lisa Madigan](/wiki/Lisa_Madigan "Lisa Madigan") (born 1966), adopted daughter of Michael Madigan, former [Illinois Attorney General](/wiki/Illinois_Attorney_General "Illinois Attorney General").

## Education

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Alex_MacLean_2005_campus_with_cityscape.jpg/250px-Alex_MacLean_2005_campus_with_cityscape.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alex_MacLean_2005_campus_with_cityscape.jpg)

Campus of the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Foellinger_Auditorium_University_of_Illinois_at_Urbana-Champaign_closer.jpg/250px-Foellinger_Auditorium_University_of_Illinois_at_Urbana-Champaign_closer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Foellinger_Auditorium_University_of_Illinois_at_Urbana-Champaign_closer.jpg)

[Foellinger Auditorium](/wiki/Foellinger_Auditorium "Foellinger Auditorium") at the [University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_Urbana-Champaign "University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign")

Further information: [History of education in Chicago](/wiki/History_of_education_in_Chicago "History of education in Chicago")

### Illinois State Board of education

Main article: [Illinois State Board of Education](/wiki/Illinois_State_Board_of_Education "Illinois State Board of Education")

The Illinois State Board of Education (ISBE) is autonomous of the governor and the state legislature, and administers [public education](/wiki/Public_education "Public education") in the state. Local municipalities and their respective [school districts](/wiki/School_district "School district") operate individual public schools, but the ISBE audits performance of public schools with the [Illinois School Report Card](/wiki/Illinois_School_Report_Card "Illinois School Report Card"). The ISBE also makes recommendations to state leaders concerning education spending and policies.

### Primary and secondary schools

See also: [List of school districts in Illinois](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_Illinois "List of school districts in Illinois") and [List of high schools in Illinois](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_Illinois "List of high schools in Illinois")

Education is compulsory for ages 7–17 in Illinois. Schools are commonly, but not exclusively, divided into three tiers of primary and secondary education: elementary school, [middle school](/wiki/Middle_school "Middle school") or [junior high school](/wiki/Middle_school "Middle school"), and high school. District territories are often complex in structure. Many areas in the state are actually located in *two* school districts—one for high school and the other for elementary and middle schools. And such districts do not necessarily share boundaries. A given high school may have several elementary districts that feed into it, yet some of those feeder districts may themselves feed into multiple high school districts.

### Colleges and universities

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of colleges and universities in Illinois](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Illinois "List of colleges and universities in Illinois").

Using the criterion established by [the Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching](/wiki/The_Carnegie_Foundation_for_the_Advancement_of_Teaching "The Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching"), there are eleven "National Universities" in the state.

As of 19 August 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Illinois&action=edit), six of these rank in the "first tier" among the top 500 National Universities in the nation, as determined by the *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* rankings: the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"), [Northwestern University](/wiki/Northwestern_University "Northwestern University"), the [University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_Urbana-Champaign "University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign"), [Loyola University Chicago](/wiki/Loyola_University_Chicago "Loyola University Chicago"), the [Illinois Institute of Technology](/wiki/Illinois_Institute_of_Technology "Illinois Institute of Technology"), [DePaul University](/wiki/DePaul_University "DePaul University"), [University of Illinois Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_Chicago "University of Illinois Chicago"), [Illinois State University](/wiki/Illinois_State_University "Illinois State University"), [Southern Illinois University Carbondale](/wiki/Southern_Illinois_University_Carbondale "Southern Illinois University Carbondale"), and [Northern Illinois University](/wiki/Northern_Illinois_University "Northern Illinois University").[[278]](#cite_note-usnews-283) The University of Chicago is continuously ranked as one of the world's top ten universities on various independent university rankings, and its [Booth School of Business](/wiki/Booth_School_of_Business "Booth School of Business"), along with Northwestern's [Kellogg School of Management](/wiki/Kellogg_School_of_Management "Kellogg School of Management") consistently rank within the top five graduate business schools in the country and top ten globally. The University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign is often ranked among the best engineering schools in the world and United States.

Illinois also has more than twenty additional [accredited](/wiki/Educational_accreditation "Educational accreditation") four-year universities, both public and private, and dozens of small [liberal arts colleges](/wiki/Liberal_arts_college "Liberal arts college") across the state. Additionally, Illinois supports 49 public [community colleges](/wiki/Community_college "Community college") in the [Illinois Community College System](/wiki/Illinois_Community_College_System "Illinois Community College System").

### School financing

Schools in Illinois are funded primarily by property taxes, based on state assessment of property values, rather than direct state contributions. Scholar Tracy Steffes has described Illinois public education as historically "inequitable", a system where one of "the wealthiest of states" is "the stingiest in its support for education". There have been several attempts to reform school funding in Illinois. The most notable attempt came in 1973 with the adoption of the Illinois Resource Equalizer Formula, a measure through which it was hoped funding could be collected and distributed to Illinois schools more equitably. However, opposition from affluent Illinois communities who objected to having to pay for the less well-off school districts (many of them Black majority communities, produced by redlining, white flight, and other "soft" segregation methods) resulted in the formula's abolition in the late 1980s.[[279]](#cite_note-284)

## Transportation

See also: [List of Illinois Routes](/wiki/List_of_Illinois_Routes "List of Illinois Routes")

Because of its central location and its proximity to the [Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt") and [Grain Belt](/wiki/Grain_Belt "Grain Belt"), Illinois is a national crossroads for air, auto, rail, and truck traffic.

### Airports

See also: [List of airports in Illinois](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Illinois "List of airports in Illinois")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Concourse_B%2C_Chicago_O%27Hare_airport.jpg/250px-Concourse_B%2C_Chicago_O%27Hare_airport.jpg)](/wiki/File:Concourse_B,_Chicago_O%27Hare_airport.jpg)

Concourse B inside [O'Hare International Airport](/wiki/O%27Hare_International_Airport "O'Hare International Airport"), one of the [world's busiest airports](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "List of busiest airports by passenger traffic")

From 1962 until 1998, Chicago's [O'Hare International Airport](/wiki/O%27Hare_International_Airport "O'Hare International Airport") (ORD) was the busiest airport in the world, measured both in terms of total flights and passengers. While it was surpassed by [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta")'s [Hartsfield](/wiki/Hartsfield%E2%80%93Jackson_Atlanta_International_Airport "Hartsfield–Jackson Atlanta International Airport") in 1998 (as Chicago splits its air traffic between O'Hare and Midway airports, while Atlanta uses only one airport), with 59.3 million domestic passengers annually, along with 11.4 million international passengers in 2008,[[280]](#cite_note-285) O'Hare consistently remains one of the two or three busiest airports globally, and in some years still ranks number one in total flights. It is a major [hub](/wiki/Airline_hub "Airline hub") for both [United Airlines](/wiki/United_Airlines "United Airlines") and [American Airlines](/wiki/American_Airlines "American Airlines"), and a major airport expansion project is currently underway. [Midway Airport](/wiki/Chicago_Midway_International_Airport "Chicago Midway International Airport") (MDW), which had been the busiest airport in the world at one point until it was supplanted by O'Hare as the busiest airport in 1962, is now the secondary airport in the Chicago metropolitan area and still ranks as one of the nation's busiest airports. Midway is a major hub for [Southwest Airlines](/wiki/Southwest_Airlines "Southwest Airlines") and services many other carriers as well. Midway served 17.3 million domestic and international passengers in 2008.[[281]](#cite_note-286)

### Highways

Main articles: [List of Interstate Highways in Illinois](/wiki/List_of_Interstate_Highways_in_Illinois "List of Interstate Highways in Illinois") and [List of U.S. Highways in Illinois](/wiki/List_of_U.S._Highways_in_Illinois "List of U.S. Highways in Illinois")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Welcome_to_Illinois_Sign_-_US67_Rock_Island_%2844320554331%29.jpg/250px-Welcome_to_Illinois_Sign_-_US67_Rock_Island_%2844320554331%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Welcome_to_Illinois_Sign_-_US67_Rock_Island_(44320554331).jpg)

An Illinois welcome sign along [U.S. Route 67](/wiki/U.S._Route_67_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 67 in Illinois") in [Rock Island](/wiki/Rock_Island,_Illinois "Rock Island, Illinois")

The Interstate Highways in Illinois are all segments of the [Interstate Highway System](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System") that are owned and maintained by the state.[[282]](#cite_note-287) Major U.S. Interstate highways crossing the state include: [Interstate 24](/wiki/Interstate_24_in_Illinois "Interstate 24 in Illinois") (I-24), [I-39](/wiki/Interstate_39_in_Illinois "Interstate 39 in Illinois"), [I-41](/wiki/Interstate_41 "Interstate 41"), [I-55](/wiki/Interstate_55_in_Illinois "Interstate 55 in Illinois"), [I-57](/wiki/Interstate_57_in_Illinois "Interstate 57 in Illinois"), [I-64](/wiki/Interstate_64_in_Illinois "Interstate 64 in Illinois"), [I-70](/wiki/Interstate_70_in_Illinois "Interstate 70 in Illinois"), [I-72](/wiki/Interstate_72_in_Illinois "Interstate 72 in Illinois"), [I-74](/wiki/Interstate_74_in_Illinois "Interstate 74 in Illinois"), [I-80](/wiki/Interstate_80_in_Illinois "Interstate 80 in Illinois"), [I-88](/wiki/Interstate_88_(Illinois) "Interstate 88 (Illinois)"), [I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_Illinois "Interstate 90 in Illinois"), and [I-94](/wiki/Interstate_94_in_Illinois "Interstate 94 in Illinois").

Illinois has the distinction of having the most primary (two-digit) interstates pass through it among all the 50 states with 13. Illinois also ranks third among the fifty states with the most interstate mileage, coming in after California and Texas, which are much bigger states in area.[[283]](#cite_note-288)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/bc/IllinoisLicensePlates.jpg/250px-IllinoisLicensePlates.jpg)](/wiki/File:IllinoisLicensePlates.jpg)

Standard license plate introduced in 2017

The [Illinois Department of Transportation](/wiki/Illinois_Department_of_Transportation "Illinois Department of Transportation") (IDOT) is responsible for maintaining the [U.S Highways](/wiki/United_States_Numbered_Highways "United States Numbered Highways") in Illinois. The system in Illinois consists of 21 [primary highways](/wiki/List_of_U.S._Highways "List of U.S. Highways"). Among the U.S. highways that pass through the state, the primary ones are: [US 6](/wiki/U.S._Route_6_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 6 in Illinois"), [US 12](/wiki/U.S._Route_12_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 12 in Illinois"), [US 14](/wiki/U.S._Route_14_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 14 in Illinois"), [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 20 in Illinois"), [US 24](/wiki/U.S._Route_24_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 24 in Illinois"), [US 30](/wiki/U.S._Route_30_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 30 in Illinois"), [US 34](/wiki/U.S._Route_34_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 34 in Illinois"), [US 36](/wiki/U.S._Route_36_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 36 in Illinois"), [US 40](/wiki/U.S._Route_40_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 40 in Illinois"), [US 41](/wiki/U.S._Route_41_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 41 in Illinois"), [US 45](/wiki/U.S._Route_45_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 45 in Illinois"), [US 50](/wiki/U.S._Route_50_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 50 in Illinois"), [US 51](/wiki/U.S._Route_51_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 51 in Illinois"), [US 52](/wiki/U.S._Route_52_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 52 in Illinois"), [US 54](/wiki/U.S._Route_54_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 54 in Illinois"), [US 60](/wiki/U.S._Route_60_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 60 in Illinois"), [US 62](/wiki/U.S._Route_62_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 62 in Illinois"), and [US 67](/wiki/U.S._Route_67_in_Illinois "U.S. Route 67 in Illinois").

### Buses

Due to its central location, Illinois sees numerous intercity bus services primarily connecting east and west. The [Chicago Bus Station](/wiki/Chicago_Bus_Station "Chicago Bus Station") is the busiest intercity bus station in the state. The following carriers provide scheduled service: [Amtrak Thruway](/wiki/Amtrak_Thruway "Amtrak Thruway"), [Barons Bus Lines](/wiki/Barons_Bus_Lines "Barons Bus Lines"), [Burlington Trailways](/wiki/Burlington_Trailways "Burlington Trailways"), [Flixbus](/wiki/Flixbus "Flixbus"), [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines"), [Indian Trails](/wiki/Indian_Trails "Indian Trails"), Miller Transportation (Hoosier Ride), [Peoria Charter Coach Company](/wiki/Peoria_Charter_Coach_Company "Peoria Charter Coach Company"), [Van Galder Bus Company](/wiki/Van_Galder_Bus_Company "Van Galder Bus Company"), and [Wisconsin Coach Lines](/wiki/Wisconsin_Coach_Lines "Wisconsin Coach Lines").

### Railroads

See also: [List of Illinois railroads](/wiki/List_of_Illinois_railroads "List of Illinois railroads")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Illinois_Zephyr_and_Lincoln_Service_trains_at_Chicago_Union_Station%2C_December_2018.JPG/330px-Illinois_Zephyr_and_Lincoln_Service_trains_at_Chicago_Union_Station%2C_December_2018.JPG)](/wiki/File:Illinois_Zephyr_and_Lincoln_Service_trains_at_Chicago_Union_Station,_December_2018.JPG)

Two [IDOT](/wiki/Illinois_Department_of_Transportation "Illinois Department of Transportation")-owned [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") locomotives at [Chicago Union Station](/wiki/Chicago_Union_Station "Chicago Union Station")

Illinois has an extensive passenger and freight rail transportation network. Chicago is a national [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") hub and in-state passengers are served by Amtrak's [Illinois Service](/wiki/Illinois_Service "Illinois Service"), featuring the Chicago to Carbondale [*Illini*](/wiki/Illini_(Amtrak) "Illini (Amtrak)") and [*Saluki*](/wiki/Saluki_(Amtrak) "Saluki (Amtrak)"), the Chicago to Quincy [*Carl Sandburg*](/wiki/Illinois_Zephyr_and_Carl_Sandburg "Illinois Zephyr and Carl Sandburg") and *[Illinois Zephyr](/wiki/Illinois_Zephyr_and_Carl_Sandburg "Illinois Zephyr and Carl Sandburg")*, and the Chicago to St. Louis *[Lincoln Service](/wiki/Lincoln_Service "Lincoln Service")*. Currently there is trackwork on the Chicago–St. Louis line to bring the maximum speed up to 110 mph (180 km/h), which would reduce the trip time by an hour and a half. Nearly every North American railway meets at Chicago (including all six [Class I](/wiki/Railroad_classes "Railroad classes") railroads), making it the largest and most active rail hub in the country. Extensive heavy rail service is provided in the city proper and some immediate suburbs by the [Chicago Transit Authority](/wiki/Chicago_Transit_Authority "Chicago Transit Authority")'s ['L'](/wiki/Chicago_%22L%22 "Chicago \"L\"") system. One of the largest suburban commuter rail system in the United States, operated by [Metra](/wiki/Metra "Metra"), uses existing rail lines to provide direct commuter rail access for hundreds of suburbs to the city and beyond, with one [line](/wiki/Union_Pacific_North_Line "Union Pacific North Line") extending into [Kenosha](/wiki/Kenosha,_Wisconsin "Kenosha, Wisconsin"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"). The [South Shore Line](/wiki/South_Shore_Line "South Shore Line") commuter service originates in [Chicago](/wiki/Millennium_Station "Millennium Station") and connects the state to [Northwest Indiana](/wiki/Northwest_Indiana "Northwest Indiana").[[284]](#cite_note-289)

The [Illinois Department of Transportation](/wiki/Illinois_Department_of_Transportation "Illinois Department of Transportation") (IDOT) has programmed several new passenger rail routes to serve the state. These include the Chicago-[Moline](/wiki/Moline,_Illinois "Moline, Illinois") [route](/wiki/Quad_Cities_(train) "Quad Cities (train)") (to be operated by [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")) and the Chicago-[Rockford](/wiki/Rockford,_Illinois "Rockford, Illinois") [route](/wiki/Rockford_Intercity_Passenger_Rail "Rockford Intercity Passenger Rail") (to be operated by [Metra](/wiki/Metra "Metra")).[[285]](#cite_note-290)[[286]](#cite_note-291)[[287]](#cite_note-292) [[288]](#cite_note-293) A route between Chicago and [Peoria](/wiki/Peoria,_Illinois "Peoria, Illinois") was accepted into the [Federal Railroad Administration's (FRA)](/wiki/Federal_Railroad_Administration "Federal Railroad Administration") [Corridor Identification and Development Program](/wiki/Corridor_Identification_and_Development_Program "Corridor Identification and Development Program") and is undergoing studies.[[289]](#cite_note-294)[[290]](#cite_note-295)[[291]](#cite_note-296)

### Waterways

See also: [Category:Illinois waterways](/wiki/Category:Illinois_waterways "Category:Illinois waterways")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Chicago_Landscape.jpg/330px-Chicago_Landscape.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chicago_Landscape.jpg)

A boat on the South Branch of the [Chicago River](/wiki/Chicago_River "Chicago River")

In addition to the state's rail lines, the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and [Illinois River](/wiki/Illinois_River "Illinois River") provide major transportation routes for the state's agricultural interests. [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") gives Illinois access to the Atlantic Ocean by way of the [Saint Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_Seaway "Saint Lawrence Seaway").

The [Illinois International Port District](/wiki/Port_of_Chicago "Port of Chicago") operates the Port of Chicago, which is located on Lake Michigan at the mouth of the [Calumet River](/wiki/Calumet_River "Calumet River"). It is the start point of the [Illinois Waterway](/wiki/Illinois_Waterway "Illinois Waterway"), which is series of rivers that allow for commercial shipping between the Great Lakes and the Mississippi River. The Illinois Waterway also consists of the [Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal](/wiki/Chicago_Sanitary_and_Ship_Canal "Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal"), which connects the [Chicago River](/wiki/Chicago_River "Chicago River") with the [Des Plaines River](/wiki/Des_Plaines_River "Des Plaines River") and replaced the [Illinois and Michigan Canal](/wiki/Illinois_and_Michigan_Canal "Illinois and Michigan Canal").[[292]](#cite_note-297)

The [Chicago River](/wiki/Chicago_River "Chicago River") hosts many tourist and commuter boat routes such as the Chicago River Architecture Tours and the Chicago Water Taxi.[[293]](#cite_note-298)
